questions.question:
When applying a stock and
flow approach, central bank monetary policy is a primary influencer of the flow of:
questions.explanation:
precious metals can be described as certain metals that act as
monetary stores of value (as well as industrial uses). Primary commodities include
gold, silver, and platinum. The primary influencers of the flow of precious metals
include central bank monetary policy, geopolitics, economic (GDP) growth. With the
lack of annual seasonality in the production of metals and ease of storage without
spoilage, much of time variability comes from the demand side of the equation (e.g.,
construction and economic growth).

questions.question:
For an investor that
estimates the impact of flows on commodity prices, geopolitical events primarily
influence the flow of:
questions.explanation:
adverse weather (cold, hurricanes) drives the change in flows
resulting from changes in season or the time of year. Primary influences for energy
commodities include: pipeline and tanker reliability, seasonality (summer/winter),
adverse weather (cold, hurricanes), automobile/truck sales, geopolitical instability,
environmental requirements, economic (GDP) growth.

EdZeb Institute

questions.question:
When estimating the
impact of stocks and flows on commodity prices, seasonality primarily influences the:
questions.explanation:
seasonality drives the change in stocks and flows resulting from
changes in season or the time of year. The demand or consumption of energy depends
upon the season (increase consumption in the winter for heating, summer for cooling).
Primary influences for energy commodities include: pipeline and tanker reliability,
seasonality (summer/winter), adverse weather (cold, hurricanes), automobile/truck
sales, geopolitical instability, environmental requirements, economic (GDP) growth.

questions.question:
A commodity investor
wishing to exploit seasonal variability in demand is most likely to trade in:
questions.explanation:
the products from crude oil and metal ore have seasonal
demands depending on weather (e.g., gasoline demand in the summer and heating oil
demand in the winter) that affect the life cycle and usage of the underlying
commodity.

questions.question:
The flexibility in industrial
metals life cycle is due to the ability to:

options.A: change the amount of ore extracted
options.B: store metals for long periods of time
options.C: increase or decrease smelting and purification

questions.explanation:
the life cycle of both precious and industrial metals is probably
the most flexible because the ore, as well as the finished products, can be stored for
months (if not years) given the relative resistance to spoilage of metals (assuming
proper storage).

questions.question:
When valuing a commodity
contract, is the settlement method likely to affect whether the law of one price will
be enforced by arbitrageurs?

options.B: Yes, it is more likely to be enforced if the contract settles in cash
options.C: Yes, it is more likely to be enforced if the contract settles using physical delivery EdZeb Institute

questions.explanation:
some commodity contracts require actual delivery of the
physical commodity at the end of the contract versus settlement in a cash payment
(based on the difference between the contract futures price and the spot price
prevailing at the time of contract expiration). The force of arbitrage—which reflects
the law of one price—may not be entirely enforced by arbitrageurs because some
participants do not have the ability to make or take delivery of the physical
commodity. In these situations, the relationships that link spot and futures prices
are not an equality but are a range that only indicates the limit or boundary of value
differences that can occur.

questions.question:
In contrast to equities and
bonds, commodity futures are primarily valued on the basis of:

options.B: supply and demand for the physical item
options.C: cash flows generated while the commodity is held

questions.explanation:
the valuation of commodities is based not on the estimation of
future profitability and cash flows but on a discounted forecast of future possible
prices based on such factors as the supply and demand of the physical item or the
expected volatility of future prices.

questions.question:
The value of a commodity
futures contract is most likely to increase as the result of an increase in:
questions.explanation:
owning a commodity incurs transportation and storage costs.
These ongoing expenditures affect the shape of the forward price curve of the
commodity derivative contracts with different expiration dates. If storage and
transportation costs are substantial, the prices for a commodity futures contract will
likely be incrementally higher as one looks farther into the future. The theory of
storage states that futures prices can be written this way: Futures price= Spot price
of the physical commodity + Direct storage costs (such as rent and insurance) −
Convenience yield.

EdZeb Institute

questions.question:
A commodity investor's
strategy is to provide insurance to hedgers in return for an expected profit. This
investor is best described as a(n):
questions.explanation:
a speculator may be willing to act as a liquidity provider, knowing
that producers and consumers may not be in the market at the same time. By buying
when the producer wants to sell and selling when the consumer is ready to buy,
speculators may be able to make a profit. In this sense, speculators are willing to step
in, under the right pricing circumstances, to provide insurance to hedgers in return
for an expected (albeit not guaranteed) profit.

questions.question:
A market participant
whose main objective is to capitalize on the mispricing between the commodity and
the futures price is best described as a(n):
questions.explanation:
arbitrageurs who have the ability to inventory physical
commodities can attempt to capitalize on mispricing between the commodity (along
with related storage and financing cost) and the futures price. They may own the
storage facilities (bonded warehouses, grain silos, feedlots) and work to manage that
inventory in conjunction with the futures prices to attempt to make arbitrage-style
profits.

questions.question:
A short position in a gold
futures contract is most appropriate for a:

options.B: speculator expecting gold prices to rise
options.C: jewelry manufacturer buying gold for manufacturing

questions.explanation:
a gold mining company, expecting to produce and sell gold in the
future, would take a short position in a futures contract to lock in a selling price and
hedge against the potential decline in gold prices.

questions.question:
When futures contracts
are in contango:

options.B: The commodity forward curve is downward sloping
options.C: The futures contract value tends to erode over time

questions.explanation:
as a long owner of a futures contract in contango, value will
erode over time as the contract pricing moves closer to the spot price, assuming all
else is unchanged.

questions.question:
If the calendar spread in
the futures market is positive, then the:

options.A: spot price is above the price of the near-term futures contract
options.B: spot price is above the price of the longer-term futures contract
options.C: price of the near-term futures contract is above the price of the longer-term futures contract

questions.explanation:
backwardation and contango are also used to describe the
relationship between two futures contracts of the same commodity. When the nearterm (i.e., closer to expiration) futures contract price is higher than the longer-term
futures contract price, the futures market for the commodity is in backwardation.
In contrast, when the near-term futures contract price is lower than the longer-term
futures contract price, the futures market for the commodity is in contango. The
price difference (whether in backwardation or contango) is called the calendar
spread. A positive calendar spread is associated with futures markets that are in
backwardation. Backwardation occurs when the near-term (i.e., closer to expiration)
futures contract price is higher than the longer-term futures contract price.

questions.question:
Which theory of
commodities futures returns fails to explain contango?
questions.explanation:
with respect to the insurance theory, Keynes (1930), the noted
economist and market speculator, proposed one of the earliest known theories on the
shape of a commodity futures price curve. Also known as his theory of normal
backwardation, this theory posits that the futures price has to be lower than the

on the price risk and provides price insurance to the commodity seller. But, markets
failed to match Keynes’s hypothesis a more sophisticated view developed to explain
futures markets in contango (i.e., when the shape of the futures price curve is upward
sloping with more distant contract dates), recognizing that certain commodity
futures markets often show persistently higher prices in the future as opposed to
the backwardation outlined by Keynes.

questions.question:
According to the theory
of storage, which of the following changes will cause the greatest increase in a
futures contract price?

options.A: A decrease in storage costs and a decrease in convenience yield
options.B: An increase in storage costs and a decrease in convenience yield
options.C: An increase in storage costs and an increase in convenience yield

questions.explanation:
the theory of storage specifies the futures price as the spot
rate + direct storage costs – convenience yield. Thus, if storages costs increase and
the convenience yield decreases, there will be an increase in the futures price.

questions.question:
A commodity investor
bought fully collateralized April contracts in a commodity on 1 March with the intent
to roll them into May contracts on 1 April. The details of the trade are presented
below.

The investor's total return for March was closest to:
questions.explanation:
Total return = Spot yield + Roll yield + Collateral yield.
The price yield or Price return = (Current price − Previous price) / Previous price. In
this case, (15.20 – 14.70) / 14.70 ≈ 0.0340.
The roll return = [(Near-term futures contract closing price − Farther-term futures
contract closing price)/Near-term futures contract closing price] × Percentage of
the position in the futures contract being rolled. In this case, [(15.20 – 14.80) / 15.20]
× 1 ≈ 0.0263.
The collateral return is the one-month return on government bonds, or 0.036/12 =
0.003.
Accordingly, 0.0340 + 0.0263 + 0.003 ≈ 0.0633 ≈ 6.33%.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the
following information about a position in a March futures contract that will roll over
50% into a June contract on 1 March:



The net roll return for February is closest to:
questions.explanation:
the roll yield or net roll return = [(closing price of near-term
contract – closing price of farther-term contract) / closing price of near-term
contract] × rollover portion = [(501 – 515) / 501] × 50% = –2.7944% × 50% = –1.3972%
≈ –1.40%.

questions.question:
An investor enters a
futures contract on a commodity at a price of $100. At the end of the month, the
futures price is $99. If the roll yield is 0.8% and the collateral return is 0.5%, the
total return for the month is closest to:
questions.explanation:
Total return on a commodity futures position is expressed as
Total return = Price return + Roll return + Collateral return. Price return = (Current
price − Previous price) / Previous price = ($99 – $100) / $100 = –1%; and Total return
(monthly) = Price return + Roll return + Collateral return = –1% + 0.8% + 0.5% = 0.3%.

questions.question:
To maintain the same
exposure in the futures markets when in contango, rolling forward the expiring
futures contracts requires buying:

options.A: a lower number of futures contracts
options.B: the same number of futures contracts
options.C: a higher number of futures contracts

questions.explanation:
if the futures price curve shape is being driven by contango—
with a higher futures price in the far contract—this scenario will require the
purchase of fewer commodity contracts than in the near position.

questions.question:
When a futures market is
in contango, the roll return on a long position will be:
questions.explanation:
in a contango market, futures prices are higher than expected
future spot prices. Therefore, when rolling over a long futures position, an investor
would be selling a cheaper contract and buying a more expensive one, resulting in a
negative roll return. The roll return is effectively the accounting difference (in
percentage terms) between the near-term commodity futures contract price and the
farther-term commodity futures contract price. When the spot price exceeds the

futures price, the situation is called backwardation, and the opposite case is called
contango.

questions.question:
If contracts in a futures
market are in backwardation, the roll return is:

options.A: negative and the investor will need to buy more contracts to maintain the same exposure
options.B: positive and the investor will need to buy more contracts to maintain the same exposure
options.C: positive and the investor will need to buy fewer contracts to maintain the same exposure

questions.explanation:
in backwardation, the longer-term futures contract has a lower
price than the near-term futures contract so that the roll return is positive when the
investor sells the near-term contract and purchases the longer-term contract. In
addition, the investor will need to buy more of the (cheaper) longer-term contract to
maintain the same exposure (in value).

questions.question:
For a portfolio manager
seeking to change their allocation to commodities, it would be most appropriate to
questions.explanation:
a total return swap is generally used by large institutional
investors (e.g., pension plans) as opposed to commodity producers or buyers. With a
total return swap, the investor seeks exposure to commodity returns, often because
of the low return correlation of commodities with other asset classes (e.g., stocks or
bonds) or as a reflection of the view that commodities provide a valuable inflation
hedge for asset/ liability matching (ALM). Therefore, such investors would engage in
a total return swap that provides them with long exposure to the future returns from
a commodity index that is used as the reference price.

questions.question:
An investor hedges
exposure to a commodity that is illiquid by using a swap contract based on another
correlated commodity that is liquid. This best describes a commodity:
questions.explanation:
a basis swap's cash flows are based on the values of two related
commodity reference prices that are not perfectly correlated. These swaps are often
used to adjust for the difference (called the basis) between a highly liquid futures
contract in a commodity and an illiquid but related material.

questions.question:
An airline hedging against
rising fuel prices would most appropriately enter:
questions.explanation:
given that oil prices are subject to many events beyond a
company’s control, a company looking to protect itself from financing risk may find
that a swap can be a valuable tool. There are many types of swaps available in the
marketplace because they are not standardized, exchange-traded contracts like
futures. In an excess return swap, the payments to either party are driven primarily
by the changes in price of each of the futures contracts that make up the index. The
net change in the prices of the underlying futures contracts is defined as the
“excess” return, and the excess return is multiplied by the contract’s notional amount
to determine the payments between buyer and seller.

questions.question:
An investor believes that
commodity prices tend to trend and wants to invest in a commodity index that is not
dominated by any one commodity. It would be most appropriate for this investor to
choose an index that is:
questions.explanation:
trending markets favor less-frequent rebalancing and equalweight indexes have more diverse holdings than production weighted indexes, which
are dominated by energy. More frequent mean reversions should favor (monthly
rebalancing), but a longer-term trend will more likely favor the annually rebalancing
indexes. In a world production value-weighting scheme that gives the largest weight
to the most valuable commodity on the basis of physical trade value energy has the
highest sector weight (historically as high as 80%).

EdZeb Institute

questions.question:
In a trending market,
which of the following index characteristics most likely leads to higher returns?

options.A: Using a floating weighting scheme rather than a fixed weighting
options.B: Rebalancing at an increased frequency rather than a lower frequency
options.C: Including a large weight of commodities that typically trade in contango rather than in backwardation

questions.explanation:
if an index uses a floating weighting scheme, such as production
value (fully or partially), then the higher (lower) futures prices usually coincide with
higher (lower) physical prices. Therefore, with this kind of approach, the magnitude
of rebalancing weights is generally lower than for a fixed-weight scheme because the
post-rebalance weights will generally drift in line with the current portfolio weights.
As a result, the S&P GSCI and BCOM indexes (using a floating weighting scheme)
typically have lower rebalancing costs and—in a trending market—have an opportunity
to outperform their fixed-weight index counterparts, particularly those that have a
relatively frequent rebalance period.

questions.question:
Which of the following
rolling methods with respect to commodity indexes would most likely result in the
highest return?
questions.explanation:
maximizing backwardation in the rolling of futures contracts of
commodities will maximize the roll return, increasing the return of the index. Using
the most liquid contract to roll forward will be using a contract that may be in
contango (negative roll yield) or not be one with the highest roll yield. Indexes that
choose (perhaps inadvertently) contracts that more commonly trade in backwardation
may appear to improve forward-looking performance (because this generates a
positive roll return), whereas those that more commonly trade in contango may hurt
performance.

questions.question:
For an investor who
wants to gain publicly traded exposure to companies that develop real estate
properties for sale, which of the following is the most appropriate investment
vehicle?
questions.explanation:
businesses are organized as REOCs, as opposed to REITs, if they
engage to a large extent in the development of for-sale real estate properties.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers
the following information about three publicly traded real estate securities:

Which of the following real estate securities most likely qualifies as a taxadvantaged REIT?
questions.explanation:
real estate investment trusts are companies that own, finance,
and—to a limited extent—develop income-producing real estate across a range of
property sectors. Most REITs are required to distribute 90%–100% of their taxable
income to shareholders. Also, in most countries, REITs are required to distribute
90%–100% of their otherwise taxable earnings [Security 1 and Security 2], invest at
least 75% of their assets in real estate [Security 1 and Security 3], and derive at
least 75% of income from real estate rental income or interest on mortgages
[Security 1]. Security 1 is best qualified as a tax-advantaged REIT.

questions.question:
The best reason to
use NAV per share rather than book value per share when valuing a REIT is that the
NAV per share is a superior measure of:

options.B: whether the REIT is fairly valued
options.C: the investments made in the properties

questions.explanation:
NAVPS is a superior measure of a company’s net worth compared
with historical book value per share.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers
the following information about a REIT:

The REIT's NAV per share is closest to:
questions.explanation:
NAV per share is calculated as follows:
Value of operating assets = NOI/Cap rate = 346,000/0.072 = 4,805,556.
To this is added the cash, undeveloped property and accounts receivable to arrive at
gross asset value: 4,805,556 + 43,000 + 38,000 + 29,000 = 4,915,556.
From this debt is subtracted: 4,915,556 – 1,440,000 = 3,475,556
Which is divided by shares outstanding to arrive at NAV per share:
3,475,556/63,000 ≈ 55.17

EdZeb Institute

questions.question:
When valuing
publicly traded real estate securities, net asset value per share:

options.A: refers to depreciated real estate value
options.B: includes the value of non-real-estate assets
options.C: provides an indication of the manager's performance relative to benchmarks

questions.explanation:
NAVPS should also include the following: the value of non–real
estate assets, including cash.

questions.question:
Compared to
adjusted funds from operations (AFFO) used in REIT valuation, funds from
operations (FFO) is:

options.A: open to less error in estimation
options.B: a superior measure of economic income
options.C: more reflective of a REIT’s dividend-paying ability

questions.explanation:
AFFO is superior to FFO as a measure of economic income and
thus economic return because it considers the capital expenditures necessary to
maintain the economic income of a property portfolio. AFFO is also more reflective
of a REIT’s dividend-paying ability than FFO. It is open to more variation and error
in estimation than FFO (funds from operations). Thus FFO is open to less error in
estimation than AFFO.

questions.question:
For an investor
interested in evaluating a REIT's dividend-paying capacity, it is most appropriate to
use:
questions.explanation:
AFFO better approximates a company’s sustainable dividendpaying capacity. Adjusted funds from operations (AFFO) subtracts from FFO
recurring capital expenditure and the difference between reported rents and cash
rents: AFFO = (FFO – Non-cash rent – Recurring capex).
36. B is Correct because, if we assume price stays the same, the P/FFO multiple will
decrease. FFO and AFFO are based on net income available to equity and thus
represent levered income. P/FFO multiples are generally lower for companies with
higher leverage, all things equal.
In addition, the perceived risk of the firm increases, causing investors to demand a
higher return. The higher required return is reflected in a lower P/FFO multiple. As
financial leverage increases, equities' FFO and AFFO multiples decrease because
required return increases as risk increases.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers
the following information about a REIT for the year just ended:

If the REIT's price-to-adjusted-FFO multiple is 12x, the forecast REIT share price
is closest to:
questions.explanation:
AFFO (adjusted FFO) is most often defined as FFO adjusted
to remove any non-cash rent and to subtract maintenance-type capital expenditures
and leasing costs (including leasing agents' commissions and tenants' improvement
allowances).
AFFO = FFO ($65,000) – Non-cash rent ($5,000) – Recurring capital expenditures
($10,000) = $50,000
AFFO/share = $50,000/20,000 = $2.50
Share price = $2.50 × 12 = $30

questions.question:
An analyst gathers
the following information about a REIT for the year just ended:

If dividends per share are $1.00 for the year, the dividend payout ratio on adjusted
FFO is closest to:
questions.explanation:
AFFO [adjusted FFO] is most often defined as FFO adjusted to
remove any non-cash rent and to subtract maintenance-type capital expenditures and
leasing costs (including leasing agents’ commissions and tenants’ improvement
allowances).
AFFO = FFO ($70,000) – Non-cash rent ($5,000) – Recurring capital expenditures
($10,000) = $55,000
AFFO/share = $55,000/25,000 = $2.20
Dividend payout ratio on AFFO = $1.00/$2.20 = 0.454545 ≈ 45%

EdZeb Institute

questions.question:
An analyst gathers
the following information (in € millions) about three REITs with the same share price
and number of shares outstanding:

Which REIT has the lowest price-to-FFO ratio?
questions.explanation:
the REIT with the highest FFO (and therefore the lowest priceto-FFO ratio) is REIT 2 (i.e., if share price is €100, price-to-FFO ratio is €100/€325
≈ 0.308 for REIT 1, €100/€330 ≈ 0.303 for REIT 2, and €100/€325 ≈ 0.308 for
REIT 3).
FFO is derived in the following table (FFO = Net income + Depreciation &
amortization).

questions.question:
Assuming similar
expected return and risk, distributions from which of the following investments will
attract the most favorable tax treatment for US investors?
questions.explanation:
one of the advantages of investing in private real estate (direct
investment) is that there are tax benefits (e.g., accelerated depreciation, deferred
taxes in some markets when sales are reinvested in other real estate).
The US tax structure allows the deduction of interest and depreciation from
property-level cash flows, prior to the calculation of income tax. These non-cash
adjustments have the effect of eliminating or deferring otherwise payable income
tax.
Further, US investors are able to defer capital gains taxes on disposition through a
special tax code provision (known as a '1031 exchange') where capital gains (profits
from sale) are not payable if the investor immediately purchases another investment
property for equal or greater value.

questions.question:
Which of the
following investments is least likely to offer exposure to real estate development
activity?

options.C: Real estate operating companies EdZeb Institute

questions.explanation:
development requires equity capital. Lenders require equity
capital to be invested in the land or construction before they fund any debt.
Therefore, a REIT would need to hold capital on its balance sheet to fund future
equity draws instead of paying out that capital. REIT laws typically stipulate that the
significant majority of a REIT's earned cash flow be paid out immediately to
investors. In contrast to REOCs, REITs face restrictions on the amount of income
and assets accounted for by activities other than collecting rent and interest
payments.

questions.question:
Compared to private
real estate vehicles, REITs most likely provide greater:
questions.explanation:
high transparency is an advantage of public real estate (such as
REITs) and low transparency is a disadvantage of private real estate.

questions.question:
Which of the following indexes would be
the most appropriate benchmark for a small, long/short equity hedge fund open to
new investment?

options.C: The Credit Suisse Hedge Fund Index

questions.explanation:
the HFRI Index is the only one of the three answer choices
that tracks small hedge funds that are open for new investment. HFR produces its
HFRI index series, which tracks only hedge funds open to new investment.

EdZeb Institute

questions.question:
A hedge fund strategy focused on mergers and acquisitions
arbitrage is best classified as a(n):
questions.explanation:
event-driven hedge fund strategies focus on corporate events,
such as governance events, mergers and acquisitions, bankruptcy, and other key
events for corporations.

questions.question:
Which of the following hedge fund strategies most
likely has the highest liquidity risks?
questions.explanation:
relative value hedge fund strategies focus on the relative
valuation between two or more securities. These strategies are often exposed to
credit and liquidity risks because the valuation differences from which these
strategies seek to benefit often are due to differences in credit quality and/or
liquidity across different securities. Also, liquidity issues surface for convertible
arbitrage strategies in two ways: 1) naturally less-liquid securities because of their
relatively small issue sizes and inherent complexities; 2) availability and cost to
borrow underlying equity for short selling.

questions.question:
The skill of long/short equity managers in delivering alpha
derives mainly from:
questions.explanation:
stock selection defines manager skill for most long/short equity
managers—with market-timing ability being an additive, but generally secondary,
consideration. Manager skill derives mainly from stock selection. Further, some
managers are able to add alpha via market timing of portfolio beta tilt, but evidence
suggests that most long/short managers do this poorly.

questions.question: Long/short equity hedge fund strategies typically have:

options.B: gross short exposure that is more than double gross long exposure
options.C: a standard deviation of returns that is more than double that of a long-only fund

questions.explanation:
long/short strategies typically have average exposures of 40%–
60% net long, composed of gross exposures of 70%–90% long, vs. 20%–50% short,
but they can vary widely.

questions.question:
A hedge fund manager is reviewing the following securities:
• Common stock of Company 1
• Non-voting stock of Company 1
• Common stock of Company 2 (a subsidiary of Company 1)
The equity market-neutral trading strategy known as 'stub trading' most

likely involves buying the common stock of Company 1 and selling:

options.C: both the common stock of Company 2 and the non-voting stock of Company 1

questions.explanation:
stub trading entails buying and selling stock of a parent company
and its subsidiaries, typically weighted by the percentage ownership of the parent
company in the subsidiaries.

questions.question: Investors with lower risk tolerance should prefer eventdriven hedge funds that:

options.C: make investments in anticipation of the announcement of a corporate event

questions.explanation:
event-driven hedge funds take one of two approaches. The hardcatalyst approach is generally less volatile and less risky than soft-catalyst investing.
Either investments can be made proactively in anticipation of an event that has yet
to occur (i.e., a soft-catalyst event-driven approach), or investments can be made in
reaction to an already announced corporate event in which security prices related to
the event have yet to fully converge (i.e., a hard-catalyst event-driven approach).

questions.question:
With respect to a merger arbitrage hedge fund strategy,
which of the following situations is most likely to be classified as a soft-catalyst
event-driven investment? The purchase of stock in a company involved in a merger
where the merger:

options.B: has been announced but not completed
options.C: is anticipated by investors but has not been announced

questions.explanation:
investments can be made proactively in anticipation of an event
that has yet to occur (i.e., a soft-catalyst event-driven approach).

questions.question:
The payoff profile for a merger arbitrage strategy
resembles that of a riskless bond and:

options.C: a short put option and a short call option

questions.explanation:
the payoff profile of the merger arbitrage strategy resembles
that of a riskless bond and a short put option.

questions.question:
In order to profit from expected mean reversion in the
yield curve, a fixed-income arbitrage fund can take long and short positions in:

options.A: the same issuer, but not different issuers within the same industry
options.B: different issuers within the same industry, but not the same issuer
options.C: either the same issuer or different issuers within the same industry

questions.explanation:
profit can be generated given mispricings in either securities of
the same issuer or securities of different issuers within the same industry.

Considering yield curve trades, the prevalent calendar spread strategy involves taking
long and short positions at different points on the yield curve where the relative
mispricing of securities offers the best opportunities, such as in a curve flattening
or steepening. The positions can be in fixed-income securities of the same issuer; in
that case, most credit and liquidity risks would likely be hedged, making interest rate
risk the main concern. Alternatively, longs and shorts can be taken in the securities
of different issuers—but typically ones operating in the same industry or sector. In
this case, differences in credit quality, liquidity, volatility, and issue-specific
characteristics would likely drive the relative mispricing. In either case, the hedge
fund manager aims to profit as the mispricing reverses (mean reversion occurs) and
the longs rise and shorts fall in value within the targeted time frame.

questions.question:
The manager of a convertible bond arbitrage strategy buys
a convertible bond and takes a short position in the underlying stock. As the price of
the underlying stock increases, the delta of this position will:
questions.explanation:
the combination of a long convertible and short equity delta
exposure would create a situation where for small changes in the equity price, the
portfolio will remain essentially balanced. As the underlying stock price moves
further, however, the delta hedge of the convertible will change because the
convertible is an instrument with the natural positive convexity attributes of positive
gamma. That is, as the price of the underlying increases, the delta of the embedded
option in the convertible will also increase. Because stock gamma is always zero, the
convertible arbitrage strategy will leave the convertible arbitrageur “synthetically”
longer in total equity exposure as the underlying security price rises. This is because
the delta of the embedded option increases, which means the sensitivity of the
embedded option price to the price of the underlying stock is also increasing. This
means increasing equity exposure for the entire strategy as the price of the stock
increases. That is, the delta of the position will increase.

questions.question:
A convertible arbitrage hedge fund manager observes the
following information about a convertible bond and the underlying stock:

The manager buys the bond and sells the stock short. If the stock price falls by 10%
over one year, the per-share profit of this strategy is closest to:
questions.explanation:
the bond value is $1,000,000 × 105/100 = $1,050,000. The
conversion price is $1,050,000/80,000 = $13.125. The profit on the long bond position
is Ending stock price – Conversion price = 0.9 × $20 – $13.125 = $4.875. This is
increased by the coupon receipt of 5% × $1,000,000 = $50,000 or $0.625 per share
(= $50,000/80,000).
The profit on the short stock position is –(Ending stock price – Starting stock price)
= –($18 – $20) = $2. This is reduced by the borrowing cost of $4 per share to a loss
of $2 per share.
Thus, the profit per share of this strategy is $4.875 + $0.625 – $2 = $3.50.

questions.question:
When considering managed futures strategies, which of the
following will most likely result in exposure that is market neutral?

options.C: Both time-series momentum strategies and cross-sectional momentum strategies

questions.explanation:
cross-sectional momentum (CSM) strategies generally result in
holding a net zero or market-neutral position.

questions.question:
A managed futures manager is considering a portfolio
allocation across three different assets with similar return expectations. The
historical standard deviation of returns is 10% for Asset 1 and Asset 2, and 15% for
Asset 3. The manager compiles the following correlation matrix for the assets'
returns:

If the manager wishes to maximize risk-adjusted returns, the largest portfolio
position is most likely to be in:
questions.explanation:
Asset 1 is the least volatile and it has a negative correlation
with both Asset 2 and Asset 3. Many managed futures managers implement their
portfolios' relative position sizing by assessing both the volatility of each underlying

futures position as well as the correlation of their return behaviors against one
another. Generally, the greater the volatility of an asset, the smaller its portfolio
sizing; and the greater its correlation to other futures being positioned, the smaller
its portfolio sizing.

questions.question:
A hedge fund implementing an equity volatility position
trade and seeking maximum liquidity is most likely to employ:
questions.explanation:
the need to maximize liquidity forces the fund to choose VIX
Index futures over over-the-counter or long-term exchange-traded VIX Index
options. In the United States, the most liquid volatility contracts are short-term VIX
Index futures contracts, which track the 30-day implied volatility of S&P 500 Index
options as traded on the Chicago Board Options Exchange (CBOE).

questions.question:
A volatility trading hedge fund strategy sells an at-themoney put option. Between the sale date of the option and its expiration date, the
price of the underlying asset is unchanged and implied volatility increases. At option
expiration, the profit on the trade is:
questions.explanation:
the short option position is left with a net gain due to the fact
that the unchanged value of the underlying asset has allowed the full option premium
to be retained.

questions.question:
A third-party broker has purchased life insurance contracts
from the original policyholders. The broker now offers to sell these contracts to a
hedge fund. An analyst notes the following characteristics of these contracts:
Characteristic I: The surrender value offered to the insured individuals is high
compared to other similar policies.
Characteristic II: Premiums payable on the policies are low compared to the relatively
high risks of terminal illness faced by many policyholders.
Which of these characteristics would most likely be attractive to the hedge fund?

options.C: Both Characteristic I and Characteristic II

questions.explanation:
the hedge fund would look for the following policy
characteristics: (1) the surrender value being offered to an insured individual is
relatively low; (2) the ongoing premium payments to keep the policy active are also
relatively low; and yet, (3) the probability is relatively high that the designated
insured person is indeed likely to die within a certain period of time (i.e., earlier than
predicted by standard actuarial methods).
Here, Characteristic II maps to points (2) and (3) above—premium payments are
relatively low while health risks are relatively high.

questions.question: Multi-strategy funds have typically:

options.C: provided better redemption provisions than funds-of-funds

questions.explanation:
multi-strategy hedge funds typically outperform funds-offunds: They have higher mean returns (7.85%/TASS and 8.52%/CISDM) and among
the highest Sharpe ratios and Sortino ratios. Multi-strategy funds have higher Rho
(more than 20%) compared to FoFs, indicating relatively high serial autocorrelation.
This is reasonable because multi-strategy funds may be simultaneously running
strategies using less liquid instruments, such as convertible arbitrage, fixed-income
arbitrage, and other relative value strategies.

questions.question:
A fund-of-funds (FoF) invests equal amounts of capital in
the following hedge funds, where incentive fees are calculated on returns net of
management fees:

If the FoF also charges a base fee of 1.0% and an incentive fee of 10.0%, the net
annual return to FoF investors is closest to:
questions.explanation:
the net-of-fees return to Fund 1 is (4.5% – 1.8%) – 10% × (4.5%
– 1.8%) = 2.43%.
The net-of-fees return to Fund 2 is (3.6% – 1.2%) – 10% × (3.6% – 1.2%) = 2.16%.
Since the FoF exposure to the two funds is equally weighted, the FoF gross return is
(2.43% + 2.16%)/2 = 2.295% and the FoF net return is (2.295% – 1%) – 10% × (2.295%
– 1%) = 1.1655% ≈ 1.17%.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following information about multistrategy hedge fund returns:

Which fund is most likely to be running strategies using less liquid instruments?
questions.explanation:
Fund 1 has the highest Rho, which indicates relatively high serial
autocorrelation. This is reasonable because multi-strategy funds may be
simultaneously running strategies using less liquid instruments, such as convertible
arbitrage, fixed-income arbitrage, and other relative value strategies. That is why,

unlike FoFs, they often impose investor-level or fund-level gates on maximum
quarterly redemptions. Also, high Rho signals smoothed returns and thus is an
indicator of potential liquidity issues (specifically, illiquidity and infrequent trading)
in the underlying securities.

questions.question:
Adding a 20% hedge fund allocation to a traditional
stock/bond portfolio would most likely result in a(n):

options.A: increase in total portfolio standard deviation
options.B: increase in the Sortino ratio for the total portfolio
options.C: decrease in the Sharpe ratio for the total portfolio

questions.explanation:
adding a 20% allocation of any of the hedge fund strategies to
the traditional portfolio almost always decreases total portfolio standard deviation
while increasing Sharpe and Sortino ratios (and also decreasing maximum drawdown
in about one-third of the combined portfolios). These results demonstrate that hedge
funds act as both risk-adjusted return enhancers and diversifiers for the traditional
stock/bond portfolio.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following information about a
traditional stock/bond portfolio and blended portfolios that combine this traditional
portfolio with an equal allocation to one of three different hedge funds:

If all three hedge funds have similar risk–return profiles, which fund is most likely to
mitigate the risk of the traditional portfolio?
questions.explanation:
the hedge fund strategy portfolios that generate the smallest
maximum drawdowns are the opportunistic strategies—specifically, global macro and
systematic futures as well as merger arbitrage and equity market-neutral strategies.
Types of strategies that provide risk mitigation for traditional assets are successful
in doing so because they are relatively opportunistic, and are liquid even during
periods of market stress.
Based on the information provided, adding Hedge Fund 3 reduces the maximum
drawdown, indicating opportunism on the part of the manager. Hedge Fund 3 also
reduces Rho, indicating a lower level of autocorrelation and therefore better liquidity
in the underlying investment strategies.

questions.question:
An investor seeks a liquid equity
exposure to real estate. The investment structure should own, manage, and develop
properties. The most appropriate investment structure is a:
questions.explanation:
real estate operating companies (REOCs) are taxable
corporations that operate and manage commercial real estate with few corporatestructure restrictions. They commonly own and often develop real estate. In addition,
REIT and REOC shares are typically liquid, and active trading results in prices that
are more likely to reflect market value.

questions.question:
For all real estate property types,
the most important factor that affects the performance outlook is:
questions.explanation:
several macro factors affect all major real estate sectors, but
GDP growth is generally the most important single economic factor affecting the
outlook for all property types.

questions.question:
Under the terms of a gross lease, a
property's operating expenses are:

options.C: shared by the owner and the tenant

questions.explanation:
a net lease requires the tenant to be responsible for paying
operating expenses, whereas a gross lease requires the owner to pay the operating
expenses.

questions.question:
A primary purpose of due diligence
for an equity investment in real estate is to:

options.B: identify problems not disclosed by the seller
options.C: verify the funding sources for the purchase of the property

questions.explanation:
the purpose of due diligence is to identify potential problems
that have not been disclosed by the seller that could negatively impact value.

questions.question:
Appraisal-based real estate indexes
tend to:

options.A: overestimate the volatility of real estate returns
options.B: lead transaction-based indexes in a falling market
options.C: lead to overestimation of the diversification benefits from private real estate. Solutions

questions.explanation:
appraisal lag tends to smooth the index, meaning that it has less
volatility. The smoothing effect will also overstate Sharpe ratios, which is

problematic if the index is used in asset allocation models to determine how much of
a portfolio should be allocated to real estate versus other asset classes. The
appropriate allocation to and benefits from private real estate would likely be
overestimated.

If the conglomerate's market value of equity is $130 million and its net debt is $10

about

A portfolio manager at an investment firm is evaluating the corporate governance

Solutions

questions.question:
Which of the following types of
dividend distributions does not affect a company's leverage ratios?
questions.explanation:
neither stock dividends nor stock splits affect liquidity ratios
or financial leverage ratios.

questions.question:
All else being equal, a stock dividend
distribution reduces a company's:
questions.explanation:
stock dividends do not affect assets or shareholders’ equity.
Although retained earnings are reduced by the value of the stock dividends paid (i.e.,
by the number of shares issued × price per share), contributed capital increases by
the same amount (i.e., the value of the shares issued). As a result, total shareholders’
equity does not change.

questions.question:
With respect to theories of dividend
policy, Miller and Modigliani argue that investors:

options.A: are indifferent between cash dividends and share repurchases
options.B: prefer a dollar of dividends to a dollar of potential share capital gains from reinvesting earnings
options.C: prefer a dividend payout ratio that is as low as possible when dividends are taxed at higher rates than capital gains

questions.explanation:
Miller and Modigliani (MM) argued that under perfect capital
market assumptions—a company’s dividend policy should have no impact on its cost of
capital or on shareholder wealth. Under the MM assumptions, there is no meaningful
distinction between dividends and share repurchases (repurchases of outstanding
common shares by the issuing company): They are both ways for a company to return
cash to shareholders.

questions.question:
For which of the following companies
would a dividend increase most likely be viewed as a positive signal by its
shareholders?

options.C: A fast growing tech company planning to raise equity

questions.explanation:
some researchers have argued that a company’s dividend
initiation or increase tends to be associated with share price increases because it
attracts more attention to the company. Managers have an incentive to increase the
company’s dividend if they believe the company to be undervalued because the
increased scrutiny will lead to a positive price adjustment. Therefore, increasing the
dividend is a positive signal for shareholders of an undervalued company.

questions.question:
A highly profitable company
with excess cash has slowing sales growth due to a lack of investment
opportunities. The best way for management to reduce agency costs is to:

options.C: maintain the dividend at its current level

questions.explanation:
increasing the dividend would reduce agency costs by returning
excess cash flows to shareholders rather than being reinvested poorly. The potential
overinvestment agency problem might be alleviated by the payment of dividends. In
particular, by paying out all free cash flow to equity in dividends, managers would be
constrained in their ability to overinvest by taking on negative NPV projects.

questions.question:
Which of the following
would best constrain management's ability to overinvest by taking on negative NPV
projects?

options.C: Set minimum levels of EBITDA coverage of interest charges as a condition for paying dividends

questions.explanation:
one managerial incentive of particular concern is the potential
private benefit managers may obtain from investment in negative net present value
(NPV) projects. The potential overinvestment agency problem might be alleviated by
the payment of dividends. In particular, by paying out all free cash flow to equity in
dividends, managers would be constrained in their ability to overinvest by taking on
negative NPV projects.

questions.question:
All else equal, which of the following
factors is most likely to result in a decrease in the dividend payout ratio?

options.B: A decrease in the volatility of earnings
options.C: An increase in profitable investment opportunities

questions.explanation:
a company with many profitable investment opportunities will
tend to pay out less in dividends than a company with fewer opportunities because
the former company will have more uses for internally generated cash flows.

questions.question:
All else being equal, a dividend
increase would be most appropriate for which of the following companies?

options.B: A profitable company with only a few positive NPV projects
options.C: A company with volatile earnings that has posted strong profits in the current year

questions.explanation:
all else equal, a company with many profitable investment

generated cash flows. Internally generated cash flow is generally a cheaper source
of equity funding than new equity issuance.

questions.question:
A corporation operates in a
jurisdiction that has a corporate tax rate of 25% and uses a dividend imputation tax
system. If the corporation distributes all its after-tax income to shareholders, the
effective tax rate on dividends of a shareholder whose marginal tax rate is 40% is:
questions.explanation:
a second major taxation system is the dividend imputation tax
system, which effectively ensures that corporate profits distributed as dividends
are taxed just once, at the shareholder’s tax rate. In this case, the shareholder's
tax rate is the marginal tax rate of 40%.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following
information about income tax rates in a jurisdiction with a split-rate tax system:

Assuming a payout ratio of 100%, the effective tax rate on pretax income distributed
in dividends is:
questions.explanation:
under this system, corporate earnings that are distributed as
dividends are taxed at a lower rate at the corporate level than earnings that are
retained. At the level of the individual investor, dividends are taxed as ordinary
income. Earnings distributed as dividends are still taxed twice, but the relatively low
corporate tax rate on earnings mitigates that penalty.
Effective tax rate on dividends = Corporate tax rate on earnings allocated to
dividends + (1 – Corporate tax rate on earnings allocated to dividends) × Shareholder
tax rate on dividends = 15% + (1 – 15%) × 40% = 49%

questions.question:
Compared to a stable dividend policy,
a constant dividend payout ratio policy would most likely result in:

options.A: gradual adjustment towards a target payout ratio
options.B: dividends fluctuating with earnings in the short term
options.C: less uncertainty for shareholders about future dividends. EdZeb Institute

questions.explanation:
with this type of dividend policy, dividends fluctuate with
earnings in the short term. Constant dividend payout ratio policies are infrequently
adopted in practice. In contrast, under a stable dividend policy, dividends over the
period were either stable or rising—even while earnings experienced considerable
variability.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following
information about a company with a stable dividend policy:

If the company uses a 3-year period to adjust its dividends, the expected increase in
the current year dividend per share should be:
questions.explanation:
Expected increase in dividends = (Expected earnings × Target
payout ratio − Previous dividend) × Adjustment factor, where the adjustment factor
is equal to 1 / Number of years to adjust dividends. In this case:
Adjustment factor = 1/3
Expected increase in dividends = (2.50 × 36% − 2.00 × 30%) × 1/3 = 0.10 = $0.10

questions.question:
Which of the following share
repurchase methods most likely has the highest cost for a company?
questions.explanation:
a fixed price tender offer can be accomplished quickly, but
the company usually has to offer a premium. Obviously, this raises the cost of the
buyback.

questions.question:
Which of the following share
repurchase methods provides a company with the most flexibility?
questions.explanation:
the open market share repurchase method gives the company
maximum flexibility. Open market repurchases are the most flexible option for a
company because there is no legal obligation to undertake or complete the repurchase
program; a company may not follow through with an announced program for various
reasons, such as unexpected cash needs for liquidity, acquisitions, or capital
expenditures.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following
information about a company that plans to issue new debt to repurchase shares:

As a result of the share repurchase, the company's EPS will:
questions.explanation:
in the case of external financing, the effect on EPS is positive
if the earnings yield exceeds the after-tax cost of financing the repurchase. The
earnings yield, the ratio of earnings per share to share price, is 3/ 40 = 7.5%. Since

this value is greater than 7%, the after-tax cost of new debt, the share repurchase
will have a positive effect on EPS.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following
information about a company:
• Current share price: $100
• Latest reported annual earnings after taxes: $45 million
• Shares outstanding: 4.5 million


• After-tax cost of debt: 7%
If the company plans to raise $25 million in debt to repurchase 250,000 shares, its
EPS after the share repurchase will be closest to:
questions.explanation:
EPS after buyback = (After-tax Earnings – After-tax cost of
funds)/Shares outstanding after buyback
After-tax Earnings minus after-tax cost of funds = $45,000,000 – ($25,000,000 ×
7%) = $43,250,000
EPS after buyback = $43,250,000/(4,500,000 – 250,000) = $10.17647 ≈ $10.2.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following
information about a company:
• Share price: $50
• Shares outstanding: 100 million
• Book value of equity: $6,000 million
If the company completes a $400 million share buyback at the current market price,
the book value per share after the transaction will be closest to:
questions.explanation:
the number of repurchased (bought back) shares = $400
million/$50 = 8 million. Then the number of outstanding shares = 100 million – 8 million
= 92 million.
The new equity book value = $6,000 million – $400 million = $5,600 million.
New BVPS = $5,600 million/92 million = $60.869565 ≈ $61.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following
information about the common stock of a company that is planning to buy back shares:

After the repurchase of 400 thousand shares at the market price, the company's
book value per share will be closest to:
questions.explanation:
when the market price per share is greater than its book value
per share, BVPS will decrease after the share repurchase. After the repurchase, the
company will have 8.6 million shares outstanding (9 million pre-buyback shares − 0.4
million repurchased shares = 8.6 million shares). In addition, its shareholders’ equity
at book value falls to $170 million ($20 × 9 million − $25 × 0.4 million). As a result, its
book value per share decreases to: shareholders’ equity/shares outstanding = $170
million / 8.6 million shares = 19.767442 ≈ $19.77.

questions.question:
Which payout methods
are best suited for distributing extraordinary increases in a company's cash flow
that are not expected to continue in future years?

options.B: Special cash dividends and share repurchases
options.C: Regular cash dividends and share repurchases

questions.explanation:
a company can use both special cash dividends and share
repurchases as a supplement to regular cash dividends. These means of distributing
cash are often used in years when there are large and extraordinary increases in cash
flow that are not expected to continue in future years. In making these types of
payments, the company essentially communicates that the distribution, like the
increase in cash flow, should not be expected to continue in the future.

questions.question:
Which of the following is most likely a
motivation for a company using share repurchases rather than cash dividends to
distribute earnings to shareholders?

options.A: To decrease the company's financial leverage
options.B: To signal that the company's prospects are positive
options.C: To create the expectation of future share repurchases

questions.explanation:
the announcement of a share repurchase program is often
understood as a positive signal about the company’s prospects and attractiveness as
an investment.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements
regarding trends in payout policies is most accurate?

options.A: There is evidence of a positive relationship between dividend initiations and enhanced corporate transparency
options.B: Globally, aggregate dividend amounts have generally increased over time while payout ratios have declined
options.C: Dividend payers are, on average, larger and more profitable companies that spend less in R&D compared to non-dividend payers

questions.explanation:
the dividend payers are, on average, larger, more profitable,
have fewer growth opportunities, and spend less on R&D compared to the nondividend payers.

questions.question:
Based on global trends in corporate
payout policies, which of the following has generally decreased over time?
questions.explanation:
research on dividend behavior globally shows that aggregate
dividend amounts as well as payout ratios have generally increased over time.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following
financial statement data of three companies financials for the most recent fiscal
year (amounts are in $ millions):

Based on the FCFE coverage ratio, the company with the greatest dividend safety is:
questions.explanation:
under the FCFE method Company A has the greatest dividend
safety.

FCFE coverage ratio = FCFE/(Dividends + Share Repurchases)
FCFE = Cash flow from operations – Capital expenditures + Net borrowing
FCFE Company A = 745 – 315 + (100) = 330
FCFE Company B = 810 – 432 + (250) = 128
FCFE Company C = 295 – 75 + (85) = 135
FCFE Coverage Ratio Company A = 330/(135 + 65) = 1.65
FCFE Coverage Ratio Company B = 128/(40 + 70) = 1.16
FCFE Coverage Ratio Company C = 135/(45 + 50) = 1.42

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following
information (in $ millions) about a company:

The company's FCFE coverage ratio is closest to:
questions.explanation:
FCFE coverage ratio = FCFE/[Dividends + Share repurchases].
Based on the provided inputs:
FCFE coverage ratio = 492 / (215 + 173) = 1.268041 ≈ 1.27x

questions.question:
Which of the following is most
likely an early signal that a company might not be able to sustain its cash dividend?

options.A: Dividends being financed by new debt
options.B: Dividend yield at the lowest historical level

questions.explanation:
analysts should be particularly alert to companies that support
their dividends and stock repurchases by reducing productive capital spending or
by adding net debt or by some combination of the two because these neither are
sustainable policies.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following
information of three companies in the same industry:

If the earnings of all three companies are expected to decline by 20% due to a
business downturn, which company has the highest risk of a dividend cut?
questions.explanation:
higher dividend payout ratios (or lower dividend coverage
ratios) often constitute a risk factor that a dividend may be cut if earnings
decline. High dividend payout ratios in relation to those of peer group companies may
also point to dividend safety concerns. Company C has the lowest dividend coverage
ratio. An earnings decline will likely reduce this ratio so that a dividend cut would be
the most likely among the three companies.

questions.question:
An analyst believes that a company is undervalued due to
a perceived lack of focus on certain business lines. The analyst would most
likely recommend that the company consider a(n):
questions.explanation:
common issuer-specific motivations to sell include focus,
valuation, liquidity, and regulatory requirements. An issuer trading at a valuation lower
than the sum of its parts is said to have a conglomerate discount, which is generally
the result of diseconomies of scale or scope, owing to a deficit in focus, management
effort, or investment; due to incompatible businesses; or because the capital markets
have overlooked the business and its prospects a divestment transaction intended to
reduce the conglomerate discount and realize that value for its stakeholders.

questions.question:
Which of the following is a type of balance sheet
restructuring?
questions.explanation:
two common balance sheet restructuring transactions are sale
leasebacks and dividend recapitalization.

questions.question:
The initial evaluation phase of a corporate restructuring
typically includes:

options.A: modeling the pro forma financial statements
options.B: determining the materiality of the restructuring
options.C: conducting a preliminary valuation of the transaction EdZeb Institute

questions.explanation:
the third question in the initial evaluation step is determining
materiality.

questions.question:
With regard to an analyst’s initial evaluation of a corporate
restructuring, which of the following are two dimensions of materiality?
questions.explanation:
an analyst’s initial evaluation of a corporate restructuring
involves answering four questions: What is happening? Why is it happening? Is it
material? When is it happening? The third question in the initial evaluation step is

determining materiality. Materiality can be defined in this context along two
dimensions: size and fit.

questions.question: A comparable company analysis:

options.B: estimates a fair takeover price for a target company
options.C: is used more often for valuing acquisition targets rather than for spin offs

questions.explanation:
a comparable company analysis is sensitive to market mispricing.
Suppose that all the comparable companies are currently overvalued by the market.
A valuation relative to those companies may suggest a value that is too high, should
the values be revised downward upon a correction.

questions.question:
Company X is evaluating the takeover of Company Y. The
CFO of Company X analyzes the following data of three comparable companies that
were recently acquired. Amounts are in $ per share.

Company Y has an EPS of $6.50. Based on comparable transaction analysis using the
mean valuation ratio, the fair takeover value of the Company Y is closest to:
questions.explanation:
this is the value based on the average takeover P/E ratio of the
three comparable companies, multiplied by the earnings of the target company.
Company 1: 21/2.50 = 8.4
Company 2: 16/1.60 = 10
Company 3: 36/3.20 = 11.25
Average takeover P/E = (8.4 + 10 + 11.25)/3 = 9.8833
Target company EPS of 6.5 × 9.8833 = 64.24 ≈ 64

questions.question:
A company acquires another company that has a similar
structure. If the acquisition is financed entirely with new equity, and both the cost
of debt and the cost of equity of the issuer are not expected to change after the
transaction, the WACC of the acquirer would most likely:
questions.explanation:
both companies have a similar capital structure, so the combined
capital structure should be similar. But considering the new equity raised, the weight
of equity in the capital structure of WACC should increase relative to the weight of
debt. Because the cost of equity is normally higher than cost of debt, WACC should
increase.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following information about two
companies:

Company A recently announced its intent to acquire Company B. To fund the
acquisition, Company A will issue 50 million new shares, raise $7 billion in new debt,


and fund the remainder with cash on hand. Company A will also repay Company B's
outstanding debt using proceeds from the debt issuance. The book value of Company
A's debt is equal to its market value. If the share prices remain unchanged following
the acquisition, the weight of debt in Company A's capital structure as a result of
the acquisition of Company B is closest to:
questions.explanation:
following the acquisition, Company's A debt amounts to $22
billion + $7 billion = $29 billion. Its number of shares outstanding will be 500 million
+ 50 million = 550 million, thus resulting in total equity of 550 million × $100 = $55
billion. The weight of debt is then $29 billion/($29 billion + $55 billion) = 0.3452 ≈
35%

questions.question:
A company seeking a modest investment in a
complementary and strategic business, along with meaningful operational control,
will most likely prefer a(n):
questions.explanation:
a joint venture typically has higher operational control than a
minority investment, but a smaller investment than with an acquisition. Joint ventures
are technically a type of equity investment (in a newly formed company) but are often
larger than equity investments in several respects: size, operational control over joint
venture, and time spent by management.

questions.question: Compared to an acquisition, an equity investment:

options.A: ensures greater control of the target
options.C: results in a larger combined balance sheet

questions.explanation:
for acquisitions, substantially greater capital investment is
required compared to equity investments.

questions.question:
A company has received a $625 million offer for its
healthcare division, which has an EBITDA of $45 million. Peer companies are valued
at an average EV/EBITDA of 14.5. The company's investment banker suggests that
the company can spin off the division at 15 times EBITDA, less 5% in flotation costs.
The best option for the company would be to:
questions.explanation:
the healthcare division has a higher valuation when compared to
peers versus either a spin off or a sale.
The offer for the company is 625/45 = 13.89 EV/EBITDA.
Comparable companies are valued at 14.5 EV/EBITDA.

A spinoff would be valued at 15 times
or 14.25 EV/EBITDA (($45 x 15 x 0.95/$45)).

less

5%

for

flotation

costs,

questions.question:
A company is comprised of two operating segments,
Segment A and Segment B. The company trades at an EV of €785 million, with an
implied conglomerate discount of €100 million based on peer valuations. Financial
information on the two segments is provided below:
• Peer median EV/EBITDA multiple for Segment A: 5.0
• Segment A EBITDA: €50 million
• Segment B EBITDA: €80 million
The company receives a bid for Segment B, valuing it at an EV/EBITDA of 5.4. The
bid:
questions.explanation:
the EV/EBITDA for Segment B is 7.9. Using the formula
Conglomerate Discount = Sum of Valuation of Segment A and B – EV = EV/EBITDAA ×
EBITDAA + EV/EBITDAB × EBITDAB – EV implies that EV/EBITDAB × EBITDAB =
Conglomerate Discount + EV – EV/EBITDAA × EBITDAA = €100 + €785 – €5 × €50 =
€635 million. Thus EV/EBITDAB = €635/€80 = 7.94 = 7.9.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following data about a
conglomerate with two segments (Segment Y and Segment Z) and compares the data
with its peers.

If the conglomerate's market value of equity is $130 million and its net debt is $10
million, the conglomerate discount (in $ millions) is closest to:
questions.explanation: the conglomerate discount is calculated using the sum-of-theparts approach as follows:

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following information (in $ millions)
about
Apex
Inc.
and
three
competitors:

Apex
management has decided to undertake a cost restructuring plan. To reach its peer
median EBIT margin level, the amount of operating cost savings (in $ millions) Apex
must achieve is closest to:
questions.explanation:
to calculate the operating cost savings required, we must first
determine the peer median EBIT margin:

The peer median EBIT margin is 30%. Therefore, Apex must reduce costs by 30% –
10% = 20%. So, to reach the median peer EBIT level, Apex must reduce costs by
$200 million × 20% = $40 million.

questions.question:
An analyst is evaluating GutWare, a European consumer
goods company that is restructuring its cost structure to increase shareholder value.
The analyst gathers the following summary financial data for GutWare and five peer
companies:

If GutWare's EBIT margin were equal to that of the peer group median, its operating
costs (in GBP millions) would total:
questions.explanation:
operating costs would need to be GBP 41,195 million to bring
GutWare’s EBIT margin in line with the peer group median EBIT margin of 23%. The
peer median EBIT margin is 23%, calculated as the median of (17, 19, 23, 25, 27). For
GutWare’s EBIT margin (calculated as EBIT/Revenues) to be in line with the peer
group median of 23% its operating profit would need to be GBP 53,500 million × 0.23

= GBP 12,305 million. Since, Revenues – Operating Costs = Operating Profit, this would
imply operating costs of GBP 53,500 million – GBP 12,305 million = GBP 41,195 million.

questions.question:
All else equal, which of the following business
model characteristics most likely results in a lower cost of capital of a company when
compared to its peers?
questions.explanation:
with regard to the relationships between business model
characteristics and a company’s cost of capital, the effect of higher earnings
predictability is a lower cost of capital.

questions.question:
An analyst researches three comparable
companies that are seeking to raise capital. Selected information from the commonsized balance sheets of the companies is shown in the table below:

Based only on this information, which company is most likely to have the lowest cost
of capital?
questions.explanation:
Company 1 has the largest percentage of cash and marketable
securities (30%) compared to company 2 (19%) and company 3 (21%).
Companies with primarily fungible (i.e., interchangeable into other units of the same
identity) and highly liquid assets, such as cash and marketable securities, are likely
to have access to lower-cost capital than companies with mostly non-fungible, illiquid
assets such as specialized property, plant, and equipment. Also, Company 1 has a lower
percentage of intangible assets (17%) compared to Company 2 (21%) and Company 3
(27%). In general, companies with primarily tangible and liquid assets are likely to be
able to access debt and equity capital at lower cost than companies with a high
proportion of intangible assets.

questions.question:
A European company has publicly-traded straight
debt outstanding that is denominated in US dollars and has a credit rating of A. When
estimating the cost of capital of the company in US dollars, which of the following is
the most appropriate estimate of its cost of debt?

options.A: YTM of the straight debt in US dollars
options.B: YTM of US dollar bonds of the company's peers with the same maturity and credit rating
options.C: YTM of Euro-denominated bonds of the company's peers with the same maturity and credit rating, adjusted for US country risk rating

questions.explanation:
if a company has publicly-traded debt with no embedded options,
otherwise known as straight debt, the yield to maturity (YTM) on the company’s
existing debt could be a reasonable estimate of the company’s cost of issuing straight
debt. Effectively, the YTM reflects the current market interest rate on the debt,
which can be interpreted as the current cost of issuing new debt with similar
features.

questions.question:
The chief financial officer (CFO) of AeroNex
Airways, a private company, considers purchasing a new aircraft for GBP 80 million.
The CFO compares two alternatives to finance the purchase: a 10-year bank loan
where the incremental borrowing rate is 9.68%, or a 10-year lease with annual lease
payments of GBP 12 million due at the beginning of each year. The residual value of
the leased asset at the end of Year 10 is GBP 5 million and the lessor would incur
direct leasing costs of GBP 2 million at lease inception. In comparison to the cost of
the bank loan, the rate implicit on the lease (RIIL) is:
questions.explanation:
the cost of the lease is 10.36%, which is higher than the bank
loan rate of 9.68%. The rate implicit in the lease (RIIL) is the discount rate that
equates the sum of the present value of the lease payments and the present value of
the residual value with the sum of the fair value of the leased asset and the lessor’s
direct costs (e.g., legal fees).

Based on the proposed terms of the lease, a financial calculator can be used to solve
for the RIIL:
Setting: Annuity due (BGN mode)
N = 10 (number of years), PV = –82 (80 cost of asset + 2 direct leasing costs), PMT =
12 (annual lease payment), FV = 5 (residual value). Solving for the interest rate (I/Y)
gives approximately 10.36%.
The cost of the lease (10.36%) is thus higher than the bank loan rate (9.68%).

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following information to
calculate the equity risk premium (ERP) for the US market:

The ERP using the forward-looking Grinold–Kroner model is closest to:
questions.explanation:
based on the forward-looking macroeconomic approach using the
Grinold-Kroner model:
ERP = [DY + Δ(P/E) + i + g + ΔS] – E(rf)
Where, DY = Expected income component = broad-based market index dividend yield
= 2.4%, ΔP/E = Expected growth rate in the P/E ratio = 1.2%, i = Expected inflation
= (1 + YTMTreasury bond/1 + YTMTIPS) – 1 = (1.05/1.027) – 1 ≈ 2.2%, g = Expected
growth rate in real earnings per share = real GDP growth rate = 2.8%, ΔS = Expected
change in shares outstanding = 0% and E(rf) = Risk-free rate = 2.1%.
ERP = [2.4% + 1.2% + 2.2% + 2.8% + 0.0%] – 2.1% = 6.5%

questions.question:
The historical approach makes use of which of the
following to estimate equity risk premiums (ERP)?

options.C: A government bond yield as a proxy for the risk-free rate

questions.explanation:
the historical approach requires an estimate of the risk-free
rate. The historical approach assumes that risk-free rates will reflect either
previous short-term rates or long-term government bond yields. A historical approach
is often used when reliable long-term equity return data are available. A historical
ERP estimate is typically calculated as the mean value of the difference between a
broad-based equity market index return and a government debt return, as a proxy
for the risk-free rate, over some sample period.

questions.question:
Three years ago, a company issued a bond using a
private placement. The company has no other debt. Six months ago, the
company undertook an IPO and started paying dividends. The company's CEO
expects constant growth in the future. Which of the following methods
is most appropriate to estimate the company's required return on equity?

options.C: The bond yield plus risk premium approach EdZeb Institute

questions.explanation:
the dividend discount model (DDM) requires that the company’s
shares are publicly traded and that the company pays dividends that are stable and
predictable. Based on these characteristics, the DDM is most appropriate in this
case.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following information about
a public company:

Based only on the data in the table, the required ROE can be calculated using which
of the following?

options.C: Bond yield plus risk premium approach

questions.explanation:
the cost of debt and the risk premium over the cost of debt are
known, so the cost of equity (required ROE) can be calculated using the bond yield
plus risk premium approach (BYPRP). The BYPRP is:
re = rd + RP, where rd is the cost of debt and RP is the risk premium over the cost of
debt. In this case re = 6% + 7% = 13%.

questions.question:
A company's preferred equity is trading at $140
per share. The annual dividend rate is 8.0% and the par value is $150 per share. The
cost of the preferred equity is closest to:
questions.explanation:
the cost of the preferred equity is (8.0% × $150)/$140 = 8.57%
≈ 8.6%.

questions.question:
A private company's capital structure is composed
of 65% in debt and 35% in equity. The company's single debt issue matures in seven
years. To estimate the company's cost of debt, an analyst summarizes the following
matrix of bond yields issued by companies with similar capital structures and credit
quality:

The estimated company's cost of debt is closest to:
questions.explanation:
the 7-year bond yield is unknown, so the analyst uses linear
interpolation to estimate its YTM. Since the spread in YTM is 7.0% – 3.0% = 4.0%,

and the range of years is 8 – 5 = 3 years, the average incremental yield per year is
4.0%/3 = 1.33%. Therefore, a comparable 7-year bond has an estimated yield of 3.0%
+ (2 × 1.33%) = 5.66% ≈ 5.7%.

questions.question:
An investor compares a company versus its
industry average, using the following data (in $ millions):

A synthetic credit rating schedule indicates:

Based on this schedule, comparing credit ratings for the company and industry
averages suggests that the company achieves a:

options.A: lower rating based on IC and a higher rating based on D/E
options.B: higher rating based on IC and a lower rating based on D/E
options.C: higher rating based on both IC and D/E

questions.explanation:
the company's interest coverage is $9 million/$ 2 million = 4.5
which compares to the industry average of $90 million/$24 million = 3.75. Therefore,
the company's rating is "A" based on interest coverage only, compared to a "BBB"
rating for the industry average. By contrast, the company's D/E ratio is $45
million/$80 million = 56.25% which yields a "BBB" rating. The industry average yields
a D/E ratio of $360 million/$900 million = 40% which corresponds to an "A" rating.
In sum, when comparing company and industry average, the company achieves a higher
rating based on IC and a lower rating based on D/E.

questions.question:
An analyst is estimating the cost of debt for a
company's 10-year non-traded bonds. She relies on an internally developed matrix
of synthetic credit ratings, a portion of which is presented in the following table:

The yield to maturity (YTM) on the US Treasury 10-year benchmark government bond
is 5.41%. In its most recent financial statements, the company reported equity of
$150.0 million and total debt of $34.5 million. The implied YTM of the company's 10year bond is closest to:
questions.explanation:
the implied credit rating is 'A' for the company and reflects a
YTM of 5.41%. The company has a debt-to-equity ratio of $34.5/$150.0 = 0.23. This
implies a credit rating of ‘A’ and a credit spread of 1.45%. Adding the 1.45% credit
spread to the US Treasury 10-year benchmark government bond YTM of 5.41%
implies a YTM of 1.45% + 5.41% = 6.86%.
