Solutions

questions.question:
A company reporting under IFRS initially classified its
holdings of debt and equity instruments as fair value through profit and loss. The
company's business model has changed, and if it bought the same securities today, it
would classify them as fair value through other comprehensive income. The company
is permitted to reclassify:

options.C: both debt instruments and equity instruments

questions.explanation:
reclassification of debt instruments is only permitted if the
business model for the financial assets (objective for holding the financial assets)
has changed in a way that significantly affects operations. Changes to the business
model will require judgment and are expected to be very infrequent.

questions.question:
When 100% of the shares in a target company are
acquired for a purchase price that is less than the fair value of its net identifiable
assets, the acquiring company should recognize:

options.B: a gain on the income statement
options.C: a gain reflected in other comprehensive income

questions.explanation:
in an acquisition of such a company, where the purchase price is
less than the fair value of the target’s (acquiree’s) net assets, the acquisition is
considered to be a “bargain purchase” acquisition. IFRS and US GAAP require the
difference between the fair value of the acquired net assets and the purchase price
to be recognized immediately as a gain in profit or loss.

questions.question:
A US company reporting under US GAAP forms a joint
venture with a European company reporting under IFRS. The two companies have joint
control of this venture. When reporting the venture's results, it is most likely that:

options.A: each company will present its proportionate share of each financial statement line item
options.B: each company will present a single line item on its income statement and balance sheet
options.C: the US company will present a single line item and the European company will present its proportionate share of each line item

questions.explanation:
both IFRS and US GAAP require the equity method of
accounting for joint ventures. Only under rare circumstances will joint ventures be
allowed to use proportionate consolidation under IFRS and US GAAP. The equity
method is often referred to as “one-line consolidation” because the investor’s
proportionate ownership interest in the assets and liabilities of the investee is
disclosed as a single line item (net assets) on its balance sheet, and the investor’s
share of the revenues and expenses of the investee is disclosed as a single line item
on its income statement.

questions.question:
A US company reporting under US GAAP forms a joint
venture with a European company reporting under IFRS. The two companies have joint
control of this venture. When reporting the venture's results, it is most likely that:

Using the partial goodwill method, the company will report goodwill (in € millions) of:
questions.explanation:
the fair value of identifiable net assets is adjusted for the
percent of the company acquired, and the goodwill recognized is the difference
between that acquisition price and the adjusted fair value of identifiable net assets.
Goodwill recognized = €280 – 70% × €350 = €35, or (€400 – €350) × 70% = €35.
Under IFRS, the parent can measure the non-controlling interest at either its fair
value (full goodwill method) or at the non-controlling interest’s proportionate share
of the acquiree’s identifiable net assets (partial goodwill method).

questions.question:
Compared to using the equity method of accounting
for joint ventures, the proportionate consolidation method results in the recognition
of higher:
questions.explanation:
proportionate consolidation requires the venturer’s share of the
assets, liabilities, income, and expenses of the joint venture to be combined or shown
on a line-by-line basis with similar items under its sole control. In contrast, the equity
method results in a single line item (equity in income of the joint venture) on the
income statement and a single line item (investment in joint venture) on the balance
sheet. Accordingly, the company's share in the joint venture's liabilities is added to
the liabilities under the company's sole control resulting in higher liabilities compared
to the equity method that results in a single line item on the balance sheet.

Edzeb Institute

questions.question:
Compared to reporting a business consolidation under
US GAAP, which of the following will be higher if reporting under IFRS using the
partial goodwill method?
questions.explanation:
the partial goodwill method will result in less goodwill being
recognized and reported, and lower total asset value being reported. Since income is
the same under either method, the partial goodwill method will result in a higher
return on assets ratio compared to the full goodwill method.
7. A is correct because the functional currency is the currency in which an entity
primarily generates and expends cash.

questions.question:
A foreign subsidiary is located in the UK and its parent is
located in the US. The subsidiary primarily conducts business in EUR but reports in
GBP. The subsidiary's functional currency is most likely:
questions.explanation:
the functional currency, is the currency of the primary economic
environment in which an entity operates. Normally, the functional currency is the
currency in which an entity primarily generates and expends cash.

questions.question:
A US-based multinational corporation has a UK subsidiary
with primary activities in Europe. The financial statements of the subsidiary for local
regulators are most likely presented in:
questions.explanation:
the subsidiary's presentation currency is most likely GBP. The
currency in which financial statement amounts are presented is known as the
presentation currency. In most cases, a company’s presentation currency will be the
currency of the country where the company is located.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following information about a
company’s transactions occurring on 15 December:
• USD 50,000 export, invoiced and paid on the same day; and
• USD 200,000 import, invoiced and will be paid in 30 days.
The company’s functional and presentation currency is EUR and the spot exchange
rates are:

Note: EUR/USD is the amount of EUR per 1 USD.
If the company's year end is 31 December, the company would report as a result of
these transactions:

options.A: no foreign currency gain or loss

questions.explanation:
transaction exposure related to imports and exports can be
summarized as follows: Import purchase - A transaction exposure arises when the
importer is obligated to pay in foreign currency and is allowed to defer payment until
sometime after the purchase date. Export sale - A transaction exposure arises when
the exporter agrees to be paid in foreign currency and allows payment to be made
sometime after the purchase date. Also, a foreign currency payable resulting from
an import purchase creates a liability exposure to foreign exchange risk. If the
foreign currency weakens, the foreign currency payable loses value in terms of the
functional currency and a gain exists. In addition, for foreign currency transactions
whose settlement dates fall in subsequent accounting periods, both IFRS and US
GAAP require adjustments to reflect intervening changes in currency exchange rates.
In this case:
Transaction 1: the export is cashed the same day the invoice is issued, thus it does
not lead to foreign exchange exposure
Transaction 2: the import leads to foreign exchange exposure since payment is
deferred for 30 days. Considering the foreign currency, USD, weakens, the company
reports a gain equal to Import value × (Spot rate as of December 15 – Spot rate as
of December 31) = USD 200,000 × (0.9525 EUR/USD – 0.9470 EUR/USD) = USD
200,000 × 0.0055 EUR/USD = EUR 1,100.

questions.question:
A company entered into a foreign currency transaction
whose settlement date falls in a subsequent accounting period. Will the unrealized
gain or loss on this foreign currency transaction be reported on the financial
statements for the current period?

options.B: Yes, it will be reported on the income statement
options.C: Yes, it will be reported in other comprehensive income

questions.explanation:
for foreign currency transactions whose settlement dates fall
in subsequent accounting periods, both IFRS and US GAAP require adjustments to
reflect intervening changes in currency exchange rates. Foreign currency transaction
gains and losses are reported on the income statement, creating one of the few

situations in which accounting rules allow, indeed require, companies to include
(recognize) a gain or loss in income before it has been realized.

questions.question:
A company has both realized and unrealized foreign
currency transaction gains. The company should report the:

options.A: realized and unrealized gains on the income statement
options.B: realized gains on the income statement and not report the unrealized gains
options.C: realized gains on the income statement and unrealized gains on the balance sheet

questions.explanation:
both IFRS and US GAAP require foreign currency transaction
gains and losses to be reported in net income even if the gains and losses have not
yet been realized.

questions.question:
Acme Manufacturing forms a foreign subsidiary and
translates its results using the temporal method. The subsidiary operates with a net
monetary liability and the foreign currency has depreciated against Acme's currency
during the most recent accounting period. As a result of the subsidiary's balance
sheet exposure, Acme will report a:

options.A: gain on the consolidated income statement
options.B: positive adjustment included in its cumulative translation adjustment account
options.C: negative adjustment included in its cumulative translation adjustment account

questions.explanation:
under both international and US accounting standards, when the
temporal method is used, the translation adjustment needed to keep the translated
balance sheet in balance is reported as a gain or loss in net income. The sign (positive
or negative) of the current period's translation adjustment is a function of two
factors: (1) the nature of the balance sheet exposure (asset or liability) and (2) the
direction of change in the exchange rate (strengthens or weakens). If there is a net
liability balance sheet exposure, and the foreign currency weakens, a positive
translation adjustment will occur.

questions.question:
A company will record a positive translation adjustment
due to a change in exchange rates when it has a subsidiary with:

options.A: a net asset balance sheet exposure and the foreign currency weakens
options.B: a net asset balance sheet exposure and the foreign currency strengthens
options.C: a net liability balance sheet exposure and the foreign currency strengthens

questions.explanation:
a net asset balance sheet exposure occurs when a company has
more assets exposed to a translation at the current exchange rate than liabilities. As
a result of the foreign currency increasing (strengthening), there will be a higher
translated amount of assets than liabilities resulting in a positive translation
adjustment (gain) for the the parent company.

questions.question:
A foreign subsidiary has the parents' presentation
currency as its functional currency. The subsidiary's PP&E will be translated at the:
questions.explanation:
non-monetary assets and liabilities measured at historical cost
such as property, plant & equipment are translated at historical rates when the
temporal method is used. A foreign entity might have the parent’s presentation
currency as its functional currency. In that situation, the subsidiary’s presentation
currency financial statements must be translated into the parent's presentation
currency as if the subsidiary’s transactions had originally been recorded in the
functional currency. IFRS describe this situation as reporting foreign currency
transactions in the functional currency. To achieve the objective of translating to
the parent’s presentation currency as if the subsidiary’s transactions had been
recorded in that currency, the following procedures are used: Non-monetary assets
and liabilities measured at historical cost [e.g., property, plant & equipment] are
translated at historical exchange rates. These procedures essentially describe the
temporal method. These procedures essentially describe the temporal method.

questions.question:
A foreign subsidiary's functional currency is the parent
company's presentation currency. The subsidiary's PP&E will be translated at:

options.A: current exchange rates with gains and losses from translation reported in the equity section
options.B: current exchange rates with gains and losses from translation reported on the income statement
options.C: historical exchange rates with gains and losses from translation reported on the income statement

questions.explanation:
if the functional currency and presentation currency are the
same, the temporal method of translation should be used. Under the temporal
method, non-monetary assets are translated at historical exchange rates. When the
temporal method is used, the translation adjustment needed to keep the translated
balance sheet in balance is reported as a gain or loss in net income.

questions.question:
Masswell corporation opens a foreign subsidiary and elects
to report the subsidiary's results using the current rate method. During the first
year of operations, the foreign currency depreciates against Masswell's home
currency. Did the choice of reporting method affect Masswell's reported operating
income?

options.B: Yes, operating income was lower than it would have been under the temporal method
options.C: Yes, operating income was higher than it would have been under the temporal method

questions.explanation:
under the current rate method revenues and expenses are
translated at the exchange rate that existed when the transactions took place. Under
the temporal method expenses related to non-monetary assets, such as cost of goods

sold (inventory), depreciation (fixed assets), and amortization (intangible assets), are
translated at the exchange rates used to translate the related assets. The
depreciating foreign currency means that under the current rate method these nonmonetary expenses would be translated at the lower average exchange rate causing
operating income to be higher under the current rate method than under the temporal
method.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following information (in GBP) as
of 31 December about the UK subsidiary of a German company that purchased
property and equipment on 1 January:



The following exchange rate information was gathered:

Note: EUR/GBP is the amount of EUR per 1 GBP.
The functional currency of the subsidiary is GBP. After translating the UK
subsidiary's balance sheet into the parent company's currency (EUR), the UK
subsidiary would recognize total assets of:
questions.explanation:
the functional currency of the subsidiary is GBP, the local
currency, and thus, the current rate method is used. Thus, assets and liabilities are
translated at the current exchange rate on the balance sheet date of 31 December.

Alternatively, Total assets = GBP 7,000,000 × EUR/GBP 1.1625 = EUR 8,137,500.

questions.question:
The foreign currency of a subsidiary has consistently
depreciated against the parent's presentation currency. The subsidiary does not have
any non-monetary liabilities, but does have long-term debt issued five years ago.
Compared to the current rate method, the temporal method of translation most
likely results in:
questions.explanation:
the temporal method will result in higher reported assets since
some of the assets will be reported at a higher historical exchange rate. The
liabilities will be the same as all liabilities will be translated at the same current
exchange rate under both methods. As a result, the temporal method will result in a
higher equity balance so that the balance sheet will balance. The current rate method
will have a negative translation adjustment due to the depreciation of the foreign
currency that will be reported in the equity section.
20. C is Correct when the subsidiary's functional currency is different from the parent's
presentation currency, the current rate method is being used and assets are
translated at the balance-sheet-date exchange rate. Therefore, the calculation is:
Total assets (translated) = Total assets in GBP × Year-end exchange rate = GBP 5
million × 1.4 USD/GBP = USD 7 million.
When a foreign entity has a functional currency that differs from the parent’s
presentation currency, the foreign entity’s foreign currency financial statements are
translated into the parent’s presentation currency. All assets and liabilities are
translated at the current exchange rate at the balance sheet date. Essentially, the
current rate method.

questions.question:
Copacabana SA is the Brazilian subsidiary of a US
corporation and its results are translated into USD using the current rate method.
In recent years, the Brazilian real has generally depreciated against the US dollar.
Does the translation process affect Copacabana's reported gross profit margin?

options.B: Yes, after translation the gross profit margin is lower than it was before translation
options.C: Yes, after translation the gross profit margin is higher than it was before translation

questions.explanation:
under the current rate method both revenue and cost of
revenue are reported using the average exchange rate for the period. As a result,
gross margins are not affected by the translation process. Revenues and expenses
are translated at the exchange rate that existed when the transactions took place.
For practical reasons, a rate that approximates the exchange rates at the dates of
the transactions, such as an average exchange rate, may be used. The current ratio,
the leverage ratios (debt-to-assets and debt-to-equity ratios), the interest coverage

ratio, and the profit margins (gross profit margin, operating profit margin, and net
profit margin) are the same in the local currency and parent currency. This result
occurs because each of the ratios is calculated using information from either the
balance sheet or the income statement, but not both.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following information (in EUR)
about a subsidiary's balance sheet at fiscal year-end:

During the fiscal year, EUR strengthens against USD. Using the temporal method
when translating the subsidiary's financial statements into USD, the company
recognizes:

options.A: a translation loss on the income statement
options.B: a translation gain on the income statement
options.C: a positive translation adjustment to shareholders' equity

questions.explanation:
the company has a net monetary liability exposure equal to Cash
+ Accounts receivable – Accounts payable – Long term debt = 400,000 + 280,000 –
200,000 – 1,400,000 = –920,000. Because the subsidiary has a net monetary liability
exposure to changes in the exchange rate, a stronger EUR results in a translation loss
when the temporal method is used. Also, IFRS and US GAAP require the resulting
translation loss to be included in net income when the temporal method is used.

questions.question:
Mandel Corp. reports under US GAAP and has a subsidiary
located in a country that may become hyperinflationary. If this happens, Mandel must
report the subsidiary's results using the:

options.A: temporal method after restating them for inflation
options.B: temporal method without restating them for inflation
options.C: current rate method after restating them for inflation

questions.explanation:
US GAAP simply require the foreign currency financial
statements of such an entity to be translated as if the parent’s currency is the
functional currency (i.e., the temporal method must be used with the resulting
translation gain or loss reported in net income).

questions.question:
A subsidiary of a European based parent company operates
in a foreign country experiencing hyperinflation. The general price level and exchange



If the subsidiary reported a capital stock of 100,000 (in foreign currency units) at
the beginning of the year, the reported value at the end of the year is closest to:
questions.explanation:
under IFRS, capital stock is first restated in terms of general
price level and then translated at the current exchange rate at the end of the period.
The restatement factor using the general price index is 2.5 (= 375/150). Therefore
the inflation adjusted foreign currency value is 100,000 × 2.5 = 250,000. The ending
exchange rate is 0.160 so the reported value is 250,000 × 0.160 = 40,000.
25. C is correct because the effect of tax rates in foreign jurisdictions rose from 2% to
4%. This greater impact could indicate that profit mix shifted to countries with
higher marginal tax rates. The change could also indicate that the marginal tax rates
increased in the countries in which it earns profits.
26. C is correct because "[c]hanges in the effective tax rate impact of foreign taxes
could be caused by changes in the applicable tax rates and/or changes in the mix of
profits earned in different jurisdictions." The effect of tax rates in foreign
jurisdictions increased from 1.8% in Year T – 2 to 2.3% in Year T – 1. If the applicable
tax rates remained unchanged, the mix of profits earned is shifting to countries with
higher marginal tax rates.

questions.question:
A company reported the following information related to
its sales growth in the most recent year:

The best estimate of its sustainable growth for the year is:
questions.explanation:
volume and price increases are 7% + 2% = 9%. Growth in sales
that comes from changes in volume or price is more sustainable than growth in sales
that comes from changes in exchange rates. Further, management arguably has
greater control over growth in sales resulting from greater volume or higher price
than from changes in exchange rates.

Edzeb Institute

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following information about three
multinational companies' sales growth drivers:

The company with the highest sustainable sales growth is most likely:
questions.explanation:
the main sales growth is from volume and price growth, which
are considered more sustainable than growth that comes from changes in foreign
exchange rates. The sales growth from volume and price for the companies are as
follows:

Hence, Company 1 has the highest sustainable growth from volume and price. For a
multinational company, sales growth is driven not only by changes in volume and price
but also by changes in the exchange rates between the reporting currency and the
currency in which sales are made. Arguably, growth in sales that comes from changes
in volume or price is more sustainable than growth in sales that comes from changes
in exchange rates. Further, management arguably has greater control over growth in
sales resulting from greater volume or higher price than from changes in exchange
rates.

questions.question:
A company based in the United Kingdom has significant
operations in the United States and in Europe. It presents the following sensitivity
analysis as part of its financial disclosures.

The potential negative impact of an unfavorable change in the USD exchange rate is:

options.A: lower than the impact of an unfavorable change in the EUR exchange rate
options.B: the same as the impact of an unfavorable change in the EUR exchange rate
options.C: greater than the impact of an unfavorable change in the EUR exchange rate. Edzeb Institute

questions.explanation:
the cash flow at risk is less for the USD than it is for the EUR.
Total exposure is the starting point for analysis. In the next stage, these exposures
are compared to all hedges that are in place. The net cash flow surplus represents an
uncovered risk position. The cash-flow-at-risk approach involves allocating the impact
of potential exchange rate fluctuations to operating cash flows on the basis of
probability distributions. Volatilities and correlations serve as input factors to assess
the relevant probability distributions.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following information about a
multinational company's additional disclosures section of the notes for a given fiscal
year:

EUR/USD
is
the
amount
of
EUR
per
1
USD.
EUR/GBP is the amount of EUR per 1 GBP.
The potential negative impact on earnings from exchange rate fluctuations is:

options.A: lower for USD than for GBP
options.B: the same for both USD and GBP

questions.explanation:
the cash flow at risk is higher for the USD exposure (being €40
million) compared to GBP exposure (being €30 million). Principal exposures are
compared to all hedges that are in place. The net cash flow surplus represents an
uncovered risk position. The cash-flow-at-risk approach involves allocating the impact
of potential exchange rate fluctuations to operating cash flows on the basis of
probability distributions.

questions.question:
A US-based company has significant operations in Europe.
If EUR strengthens relative to USD, after translating the European revenues into
USD, the company's reported revenues most likely:
questions.explanation:
if the Euro strengthens relative to USD, the European revenues
in EUR will be translated into an higher amount of USD revenues and hence overall
increase the company's reported revenues. For a multinational company, sales growth
is driven not only by changes in volume and price but also by changes in the exchange
rates between the reporting currency and the currency in which sales are made.

questions.question:
Share-based
compensation with a multi-year vesting period is:

options.B: recognized as an expense equal to the present value of the estimated share value at settlement date
options.C: measured at the fair value as of the grant date and recognized as an expense over the vesting period

questions.explanation:
for a given share-based compensation, the amount recognized
over the vesting period equals the fair value, measured on the grant date. Also, for
share-based compensation, fair value is measured only once, at the grant date. Any
subsequent change in the fair value has no effect.

Edzeb Institute

questions.question:
Which of the following
is classified as short-term employee compensation?
questions.explanation: contributions to social security schemes are considered shortterm benefits.

questions.question:
When restricted stock
units vest, the accounting entries include:

options.A: a transfer between two balance sheet accounts
options.B: a compensation charge on the income statement
options.C: an adjustment on the statement of cash flows when using the direct method. Edzeb Institute

questions.explanation:
when restricted stock units (RSUs) vest, settlement occurs
automatically, converting to common stock. The only accounting entry required is
transferring amounts from the share-based compensation reserve account to common
stock and paid-in capital accounts on the balance sheet.

questions.question:
On the date that stock
options are exercised, is the transaction recorded on the statement of cash flows?

options.B: Yes, as a decrease to financing activities
options.C: Yes, as an increase to financing activities

questions.explanation:
when options vest, settlement does not occur until the options
are exercised, which is at the employee’s discretion and depends on the share price.
At settlement, a cash inflow is recorded in financing activities on the statement of
cash flows for the number of options exercised multiplied by the strike price.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the
following information about a company:

For use in a discounted cash flow model, the most appropriate estimate of free cash
flow per share is closest to:
questions.explanation:
the model should deduct share-based compensation from free
cash flow and use diluted shares. In this case, ($32,340 – $1,147) / 1,951 ≈ $15.99.
Since share-based compensation is non-cash, discounted cash flow models used to
value companies and their equity do not account for it by default. Accordingly, we
need to modify the model to account for the effect of dilution from outstanding but
unvested share-based awards and dilution from future share-based awards.
Accounting for the first effect is straightforward: use diluted shares outstanding.
The most pragmatic method to account for the second effect, dilution from expected
future share-based awards, in a discounted cash flow valuation is to deduct sharebased compensation from free cash flow.

questions.question:
An analyst adjusts the
dilution impact of future share-based awards on valuation. The most appropriate way
to model this impact is to:

options.A: exclude the future awards from valuation until granted and vested
options.B: deduct the expense of future share-based awards from future free cash flows
options.C: decrease the number of shares outstanding by the number of unvested shares awarded when computing diluted EPS

questions.explanation:
the most pragmatic method to account for the second effect,
dilution from expected future share-based awards, in a discounted cash flow
valuation is to deduct share-based compensation from free cash flow.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the
following information about a company's defined benefit pension plan:

If no plan contributions were made during the financial year, the year-end financial
statements recognize:

options.B: an operating cash outflow of £12 million
options.C: other comprehensive income of £14 million. Edzeb Institute

questions.explanation:
the pension expense has three components, two recognized on
the income statement and one recognized in other comprehensive income (OCI).
1. Service costs, recognized as an operating expense on the income statement
2. Net interest expense/income, calculated by multiplying the net pension liability
or net pension asset at the beginning of the period by the discount rate. Net
interest expense/income is recognized below the operating income line on the
income statement, along with other financing costs like interest cost on debt
3. Remeasurements, recognized in OCI, not in earnings.
Thus, the amount recognized as an operating expense equals the service costs.
Pension expense = Service costs + Net interest expense + Remeasurements
Net interest expense = Net pension liability at the beginning of the period × discount
rate = 100 × 5% = 5.
Rearranging, Service costs = Pension expense – Net interest expense –
Remeasurements = 12 – 5 – (–3) = 12 – 5 + 3 = 10.

Edzeb Institute

questions.question:
When conducting a
discounted cash flow valuation of a company that has a defined benefit pension plan,
it is most appropriate to:

options.A: add the funded status of an underfunded plan to debt
options.B: subtract the funded status of an overfunded plan from debt
options.C: subtract future service and interest costs from free cash flow

questions.explanation:
to account for the first impact, analysts apply an asymmetrical
treatment of underfunded and overfunded plans. The funded status for an
underfunded plan is considered debt in an enterprise value calculation and/or bridge
from enterprise value to equity value. An overfunded plan is ignored in valuation.

questions.question:
An analyst is building a
financial model for a company. With respect to the company’s defined benefit plans,
the analyst should adjust:

options.A: free cash flow by adding back service cost
options.B: free cash flow by subtracting net interest expense
options.C: enterprise value by subtracting the net pension liability

questions.explanation:
the funded status for an underfunded plan is considered debt
in an enterprise value calculation.

questions.question:
An analyst is evaluating the financial reports
of three GAAP-compliant companies and makes the following observations:
• Company 1's earnings are affected by biased accounting choices.
• Company 2's earnings do not provide an adequate level of return.
• Company 3's earnings appear to be managed to result in lower earnings volatility.
Which company should be considered to offer the highest financial reporting quality?
questions.explanation:
the hierarchy of financial reporting quality is:
1.
2.
3.
4.
5.
6.

GAAP-compliant, decision useful, sustainable and adequate returns
GAAP, decision useful, but unsustainable or inadequate earnings (Company
Within GAAP, but biased accounting choices (Company 1)
Within GAAP, but "earnings management" (Company 3)
Non-compliant accounting
Fictitious transactions

questions.question:
Decisions in financial reporting that increase
earnings in the current period and are likely to decrease earnings in subsequent
periods are best described as:
questions.explanation:
choices are deemed to be “aggressive” if they increase the
company’s reported performance and financial position in the current period.
Aggressive choices may decrease the company’s reported performance and financial
position in later periods.

questions.question:
Understatement of contingent liabilities
is most likely associated in the current period with understated:
questions.explanation:
following are some examples of choices—accounting choices that
comply with GAAP, accounting choices that depart from GAAP, and operating
choices—and their effects in the current period. Understatement of contingent
liabilities is associated with overstated equity resulting from understated expenses
and overstated income or overstated other comprehensive income.

questions.question:
A portfolio manager analyzes a company by
taking the following actions:
• Action 1: Learn about the company's management
• Action 2: Comparing the company’s accounting policies with those of its closest
competitors
Which of the above actions is useful for evaluating the quality of a company’s financial
reports?
questions.explanation:
both Action 1 and Action 2 are useful for evaluating the quality
of a company's financial reports. General Steps to evaluate the quality of financial
reports include learning about management, evaluating whether the company's
management has any particular incentive to misreport, and reviewing disclosures
about compensation and insider transactions, especially insider sales of the company's
stock. When reviewing the disclosures concerning related party transactions, make
comparisons by comparing the company’s accounting policies with those of its closest
competitors and determine if there are significant differences. If so, determine the
directional effect of the differences. Therefore, both Action 1 and Action 2 are
useful for evaluating the quality of a company’s financial reports.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the Beneish model Mscores below and uses a −1.78 M-score cutoff.

Based on this information, which company has the highest probability of earnings
manipulation?
questions.explanation:
Company 2's M-score of −1.50 is higher than −1.78. Higher Mscores (i.e., less negative numbers) indicate an increased probability of earnings
manipulation. Therefore, using a −1.78 M-score cutoff, Company 2 shows an increased
probability of manipulation. Compared to Company 1 and 3, which have M-scores below
the cutoff, Company 2 has the highest probability of earnings manipulation.

Edzeb Institute

questions.question:
Which of the following best explains why
the predictive power of the Beneish model is declining over time?

options.A: The model using accounting numbers is a partial representation of economic reality
options.B: Earnings manipulators are aware of the power of the model to screen possible cases of earnings manipulation
options.C: Financial models based on accounting numbers are only capable of establishing associations between variables

questions.explanation:
earnings manipulators are just as aware as analysts of the power
of quantitative models to screen for possible cases of earnings manipulation. The
predictive power of the Beneish model is declining over time. Undoubtedly, many
managers have learned to test the detectability of earnings manipulation tactics by
using the model to anticipate analysts’ perceptions.

questions.question:
Based on the Beneish model M-scores below,
which company has the highest probability of earnings manipulation?
questions.explanation:
higher M-scores (i.e., less negative numbers) indicate an
increased probability of earnings manipulation. The probability is given by the amount
in the left side of the distribution. Beneish considered that the likely relevant cutoff
for investors is a probability of earnings manipulation of 3.8% (an M-score exceeding
−1.78). The company with the highest M-score in the current year is Company Z, with
an M-score of −1.50. Company X has the lowest M-score at −2.01, and Company Y also
has a lower M-score at −1.86. The M-score for Company Z is above the relevant cutoff
of −1.78.

questions.question:
The only factor expected to give rise to
normal accruals in a company’s earnings is the growth in credit sales. If there is no
residual when total accruals are regressed on the growth in credit sales, total
accruals are most likely equal to:
questions.explanation:
a company’s normal accruals are modeled as a function of
economic factors, such as growth in credit sales and the amount of depreciable
assets. Growth in credit sales would be expected to result in accounts receivable
growth, and depreciable assets would be associated with the amount of depreciation.
To apply this approach, total accruals are regressed on the factors expected to give
rise to normal accruals, and the residual of the regression would be considered a
proxy for abnormal accruals. If the residual of the regression is zero, the total
accruals would equal the normal accruals (also called “non-discretionary” accruals). An
important distinction is between accruals that arise from normal transactions in the
period (called “non-discretionary”) and accruals that result from transactions or
accounting choices outside the normal, which are possibly made with the intent to
distort reported earnings (called “discretionary accruals”).

questions.question:
An analyst assesses the earnings of three
companies using a regression model, Earningst+1 = α + β1 × Earningst + ε. The analyst
determines the following parameters:

The company showing the highest earnings persistence is most likely:
questions.explanation:
persistence can be expressed as the coefficient on current
earnings in a simple model:
Earningst+1 = α + β1Earningst + ε A higher coefficient (β1) represents more persistent
earnings. Company 1 has the highest β1 (0.8) among the 3 companies thus showing the
highest earnings persistence.

questions.question:
Which of the following is considered as an
after-the-fact confirmation of poor earnings quality?

options.A: Restatements related to earnings management only
options.B: Enforcement actions related to earnings management only
options.C: Both restatements and enforcement actions related to earnings management Edzeb Institute

questions.explanation:
enforcement actions and restatements are after-the-fact
confirmations of poor-quality earnings. From an analyst’s perspective, after-the-fact
evidence of earnings management is not particularly useful for anticipating issues
with earnings quality.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following information
about a company:

The company’s recurring or core pretax earnings is:
questions.explanation:
recurring or core pre-tax earnings would be $3.0 billion, which
is calculated as follows:
Profit before tax + non-recurring expenses
= $2.0 billion + acquisitions and divestiture expenses + restructuring expenses

= $2.0 billion + $0.6 billion + $0.4 billion
= $3.0 billion

questions.question:
Earnings most likely show the fastest
reversion to the mean if:

options.A: earnings have a significant accruals component and the accrual elements are normal
options.B: earnings have a significant accruals component and the accrual elements are outliers
options.C: earnings are dominated by the cash flow component and the accruals component is insignificant

questions.explanation:
if earnings have a significant accruals component, it may hasten
the earnings’ reversion to the mean, even more so when the accrual elements are
outliers relative to the normal amount of accruals in a company’s earnings.

questions.question:
Which of the following practices involve
inappropriate transfers of resources between public companies and private entities
owned by their managers?
questions.explanation:
both propping and tunnelling are practices involving
inappropriate transfer of resources between public companies and private entities
owned by their managers. The manager-owned entity could transfer resources to the
public company to ensure its economic viability and thus preserve the option to
misappropriate or to participate in profits in the future. These practices are often
referred to as propping. Dealings between a public company and the manager-owned
entity might take place at prices that are unfavorable for the public company in order
to transfer wealth from the public company to the manager-owned entity. These
practices are often referred to as tunneling.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following information
about a company's software sales in the beginning of the most recent year:

What is the most appropriate revenue to be recognized in the most recent year?
questions.explanation:
for multiple-deliverable arrangements, product revenue is
usually recognized immediately whereas service revenue is recognized as the services
are provided. Following the principle, the calculation for the year of sale is:

Software license component + Support service component / Contract period
= $3.6 million + $2.4 million / 3 years
= $3.6 million + $0.8 million = $4.4 million
For multiple-deliverable arrangements, recognition of revenue on a software
delivery is only permitted if the software sale could be separated from the
service portion of the contract.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers information about two
companies to assess the quality of their cash flow:

Which company shows a potentially poor reporting quality?
questions.explanation:
Company 2 shows evidence of potential earnings manipulation
which is a misrepresentation of reality and thus an example of poor reporting quality.
Cash flow can be described as low quality either because the reported information
correctly represents bad economic performance (poor results quality) or because the
reported information misrepresents economic reality (poor reporting quality). Large
differences between earnings and operating cash flow or increases in such
differences can be an indication of earnings manipulation.

questions.question:
Management can choose to inflate operating
cash flows by:

options.C: reclassifying interest paid from financing to operating

questions.explanation:
quality issues with cash flow reporting can exist. One issue that
arises with regard to cash flow reporting quality is timing. For example, by selling
receivables to a third party (decreasing accounts receivable) and/or by delaying
paying its payables, a company can boost operating cash flow. Thus, an analyst can
potentially detect management choices to decrease current assets or increase
current liabilities, choices that will increase operating cash flow, by looking at asset
utilization (activity) ratios, changes in balance sheet accounts, and disclosures in
notes to the financial statements.

Edzeb Institute

questions.question:
Which of the following reclassifications
is most likely to increase the operating cash flows of a company reporting under
IFRS?
• Reclassification 1: Reclassifying interest received from operating to investing
• Reclassification 2: Reclassifying interest paid from operating to financing
questions.explanation:
if an IFRS-reporting company that changed its classification of
interest paid from operating to financing, the company’s operating cash flow would
appear higher than the prior period even if no other activities occurred in the period.

questions.question:
Which of the following is most likely to
positively affect operating cash flows?

options.C: Both increasing days of payables and increasing days sales outstanding

questions.explanation:
by delaying paying its payables, a company can boost operating
cash flow (OCF). An increase in such activities would be reflected as an increase in
the company’s days of payables.

questions.question:
A company understating the valuation
allowance for deferred tax assets:

options.A: overstates both tax expenses and the value of the deferred tax assets
options.B: overstates tax expenses and understates the value of the deferred tax assets
options.C: understates tax expenses and overstates the value of the deferred tax assets. Edzeb Institute

questions.explanation:
the understatement of valuation allowance for deferred tax
assets would understate tax expenses and overstate the value of the assets on the
balance sheet.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following information
about a take-or-pay contract disclosed by a company in the notes to its financial
statements:

To properly reflect the company's leverage, using constructive capitalization, the
analyst should adjust the company's reported financial statements by:

options.A: adding €8,000,000 to the assets and adding €8,000,000 to the liabilities
options.B: adding €10,000,000 to the assets and adding €10,000,000 to the liabilities
options.C: adding €8,000,000 to the assets, adding €10,000,000 to the liabilities and subtracting €2,000,000 from equity

questions.explanation:
one common source of off-balance-sheet obligation is purchase
contracts, which may be structured as take-or-pay contracts. Analysts typically
adjust reported financial statement information by constructively capitalizing, where
material, purchase obligations. Constructive capitalization means that the analyst
estimates the amount of the obligation as the present value of future purchase
obligation payments and then adds the amount of the obligation to the company’s
reported assets and liabilities. In this case, the analyst should add €8,000,000 to the
company’s reported assets and liabilities.

questions.question:
A company has a substantial portion of its
assets valued using non-observable inputs. Which of the following aspects of financial
reporting quality is most likely to be adversely impacted?
questions.explanation:
an important aspect of financial reporting quality for the
balance sheet is unbiased measurement. The balance sheet of a company with a
substantial portion of its assets valued using non-observable inputs likely warrants
closer scrutiny.

questions.question:
Which of the following bankruptcy
prediction models best addresses the issue of the going-concern assumption in using
historical financial statements?
questions.explanation:
another shortcoming of the Altman model, and other accountingbased bankruptcy prediction models, is that financial statements measure past
performance and incorporate the going-concern assumption. The reported values on
a company’s balance sheet assume that the company is a going concern rather than
one that might be failing. An alternative is to use market-based bankruptcy prediction
models.

questions.question:
Based on the Altman model Z-scores below,
which company is most likely to have the highest probability of bankruptcy?
questions.explanation:
the interpretation of the score is that a higher Z-score is
better. In Altman’s application of the model, scores of less than 1.81 indicated a high
probability of bankruptcy, scores greater than 3.00 indicated a low probability of
bankruptcy, and scores between 1.81 and 3.00 were not clear indicators. The company
with the lowest Z-score in the current year is Company A, with a Z-score of 1.6.
Company C has the highest Z-score at 3.2, and Company B also has a higher Z-score
at 2.4. The Z-score for Company A is below the relevant cutoff of 1.81 which indicates
a high probability of bankruptcy.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following information
about three companies:

Possible reporting problems are least likely signaled in relation to:
questions.explanation:
as long as mandatory rotation rules are complied with,

change in the auditor (especially multiple changes in the auditor) can signal possible
reporting problems.

questions.question:
An analyst makes the following statements
about disadvantages of using the audit opinion as a source of information about
reporting risk:
Statement 1: The audit opinion is unlikely to provide timely information
Statement 2: Internal controls over financial reporting are out of scope of audit
opinions
Which of the above statements is most accurate?
questions.explanation:
an audit opinion(s) covering financial statements (and internal
controls over financial reporting, where required) can provide some information about
reporting risk. However, the content of an audit opinion is unlikely to be a timely
source of information about risk.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the
following information about a company:

Which of the company's accruals ratios have improved?

options.C: Both the cash flow accruals ratio and the balance sheet accruals ratio

questions.explanation:
when examining accruals ratios, it would be a concern if the
absolute levels of the accruals ratio were high and if they were consistently trending
higher. The cash flow accruals ratio improved from –6.7% to –1.4% with a lower
absolute value, while the balance sheet accruals ratio deteriorated from 1.3% to 9.8%
with a higher absolute value.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the
following information (in € thousands) about a company:


Which of the following is the most appropriate measure of profit generated by the
company's own asset base?
questions.explanation:
it exclude income from associates and joint ventures only, i.e.,
€15,080 – €6,086 = €8,994. The necessary modifications to the reported financial
data to isolate these effects includes subtracting income from associates from the
net income (profit for the year) gives the profits generated by its own asset base.
With this information, the analyst can assess the profitability and returns of the
largest and most relevant part of the entire entity: the core company. The provisions
and impairment charges should not be excluded, as provisions and impairment charges
involve decisions by management, they recur regularly, and they affect the returns
to shareholders. In assessing the company’s prospects. These charges are important
variables that should not to be ignored. Subtracting income from associates from the
net income (profit for the year) gives the profits generated by Nestlé’s own asset
base.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the
following information about a company (in € millions):

When adjusting financial statements to analyze the performance of core operations,
the best practice is to use a profit before taxes (in € millions) of:
questions.explanation:
unlike the core operations and resources, the returns earned
by associates are not under the direct control of management. To avoid making
incorrect inferences about the profitability of operations, the analyst wants to
remove the effects of the investments in associates from the balance sheet and
income statement. In this case, the best measure of performance of operations is
determined by adjusting the profit before taxes with the income from associates
and joint ventures = Profit before taxes – Income from associates and joint ventures
= 76 – 50 = 26 (also equal to the Profit before taxes, associates, and joint ventures).

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the
following information about a company's financial reporting quality:

Which of the above ratios shows an improvement?

options.C: Both cash-flow-based accruals and balance-sheet-based accruals ratios

questions.explanation:
both balance-sheet-based and cash-flow-based accruals are
insignificant and they are improving in absolute levels from 3% in the prior year to
1% in the current year. The cash-flow-based accruals are negative and increasing but
it is their absolute level that is relevant in this case. It would be a concern if the
absolute levels of the accruals ratio were high; even more worrisome would have been
if they were consistently trending higher which is not the case here.

Edzeb Institute

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the
following information (in € thousands) about a company:

The company's cash flow accruals ratio is closest to:
questions.explanation:
this is the correct calculation for the cash flow accruals ratio.
Cash flow accruals ratio for time t
= [NIt − (CFOt + CFIt)] / [(NOAt + NOAt−1)/2]
where [NOA is net operating assets,] NI is net income, CFO is cash flow from
operations, and CFI is cash flow from investing.
Cash flow accruals ratio for time t
= [NIt − (CFOt + CFIt)] / [(NOAt + NOAt−1)/2]
= [15,200 − (14,800 – 2,900)] / 82,500
= 3,300 / 82,500
= 4%

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the
following information (in € millions) about a company:

The company's balance sheet accruals ratio for the most recent year is closest to:
questions.explanation:
this is the correct calculation for the balance sheet accruals
ratio. Balance sheet accruals ratio for time t
= (NOAt − NOAt −1) / [(NOAt + NOAt−1) / 2]
where NOA is Net Operating Assets.
= (84 − 76) / [(84 + 76) / 2]
= 8 / 80
=10%

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the
following information about a company (in € millions):

The adjusted profit before tax that best reflects the company's core performance
(in € millions) is:
questions.explanation:
the analyst is interested in the ongoing operations of the
company unaffected by non-repeating types of gains or losses (such as One-off
income from associates and joint ventures and One-off revaluation loss).

Accordingly, the Adjusted profit before tax of core operations =
Profit before tax – One-off income from associates and joint ventures + Oneoff revaluation loss = 30 – 10 + 4 = 24.
Recurring yearly provisions for restructuring activities are already reflected
in Profit before tax and no adjustment is needed. Provisions involve decisions
by management, they recur regularly, and they affect the returns to
shareholders. In assessing the company’s prospects, these charges are
important variables that should not to be ignored.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the
following information (in € millions) about a company:

The company classifies its interest expenses as financing cash flows. The ratio of
questions.explanation:
net operating cash flows has to be adjusted for interest and
taxes in order to be comparable to EBIT. As the company classifies interest expenses
as financing cash flows, the only necessary adjustment is for taxes. The operating
cash flow before interest and taxes = 97 + 17 = 114. Dividing this by EBIT of 80 yields
1.425 ≈ 1.4.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the
following information about a company:

Which of the segments is the company most likely growing?
questions.explanation:
for Segment C, its percentage of total capital expenditures is
36% while its percentage of total assets is 30%, yielding a ratio of 36% / 30% = 1.20.
When comparing a segment's proportion of capital expenditures to its proportion of
total assets. A ratio of greater than 1 indicates the company is growing the segment;
the segment is receiving a “growth allocation” of capital spending.

Edzeb Institute

questions.question:
Which of the following statements
is most accurate about assets on the balance sheets of commercial banks for financial
reporting purposes? The assets of commercial banks:

options.B: are often measured at fair market value for financial reporting
options.C: are adjusted with regulatory risk-weights when reporting total assets on balance sheet

questions.explanation:
a distinctive feature of financial institutions is that their assets
are predominantly financial assets, such as loans and securities. In contrast, the
assets of most non-financial companies are predominantly tangible assets. Unlike
many tangible assets, financial assets are often measured at fair market value for
financial reporting.

questions.question:
Which of the following entities is most
likely considered systemically important by government and regulators?

options.C: An international property and casualty insurer

questions.explanation:
a distinctive feature of financial institutions, in particular,
banks, is their systemic importance, which means that their smooth functioning is
essential to the overall health of an economy. The network of inter-linkages across
entities means that the failure of one bank will negatively affect other financial and
non-financial entities. The larger the bank and the more widespread its inter-linkages,
the greater its potential impact on the entire financial system.

questions.question:
An analyst is evaluating three different funding
sources with similar residual maturities between six months and one year. Which of
the following is considered to be the most stable funding source under Basel III?

options.A: Funding provided by central banks and financial institutions
options.B: Less stable non-maturity and term deposits provided by retail and small business customers
options.C: Funding from sovereigns, public sector entities, and multilateral and national Edzeb Institute

questions.explanation:
Basel III requires a bank to have a minimum amount of stable
funding relative to the bank’s liquidity needs over a one-year horizon. Stability of
funding is based on the tenor of deposits (e.g., longer-term deposits are more stable
than shorter-term deposits) and the type of depositor (e.g., funds from consumers’
deposits are considered more stable than funds raised in the interbank markets).

Under Basel III, the available stable funding is determined by assigning a
bank’s capital and liabilities to one of five categories. The amount assigned to
each category is then multiplied by an available stable funding (ASF) factor,
and the total available stable funding is the sum of the weighted amounts. Less
stable non-maturity deposits and term deposits with residual maturity of less
than one year provided by retail and small business customers has an ASF
factor of 90%, which is the highest among the three choices.

questions.question:
Which of the following requirements under Basel
III most likely ensures a bank’s liquidity needs are met over a 1-year horizon?
questions.explanation:
the minimum amount of stable funding is relative to the bank’s
liquidity needs over a one-year horizon

questions.question:
With respect to a bank's sensitivity to market
risk, covariance adjustment reflects that its total VaR on a given day is:

options.A: less than the sum of the VaRs relating to each individual risk type
options.B: equal to the sum of the VaRs relating to each individual risk type
options.C: greater than the sum of the VaRs relating to each individual risk type

questions.explanation:
covariance adjustment reflects the fact that the risks within
each and across risk types are not perfectly correlated and, consequently, the total
VaR on a given day will be lower than the sum of the VARs relating to each individual
risk type.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following information
regarding a bank's capital ratios:

The Tier 2 capital ratio is:
questions.explanation:
Total Capital Ratio equals Tier 1 Capital plus Tier 2 Capital.

Rearranging the above formula, Tier 2 Capital Ratio
= Total Capital Ratio – Tier 1 Capital Ratio
= 7.9% – 5.7%
= 2.2%

Edzeb Institute

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following information about
three banks:

Which of the above banks is most likely to have a culture that is risk averse and
cautious?
questions.explanation:
a bank’s culture may be very risk averse and cautious and make
only loans perceived to be low risk, or alternatively, it may be risk seeking and willing
to take risk in pursuit of high returns on investment. A bank investor can qualitatively
assess a bank’s cultural environment by considering factors such as the bank’s
experience with loss reserves and if the bank has been frequently slow to provide for
losses, and only to record large asset write-downs later. A bank showing a risk seeking
culture is slow to provide for losses by postponing recognition of bad loans to
maximize performance, incentives in the short run and also hoping that conditions
would improve in the future and decrease the need for loss recognition. When
expectations fail to come true, large asset write-downs occur. The opposite applies
for risk averse and cautious risk cultures.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following information about
a bank:

The bank's liquidity position has most likely:
questions.explanation:
to assess the bank's liquidity position, the Net Stable Funding
Ratio (which improved) is most relevant, and not the Tier 1 Capital Ratio (which
remained unchanged) or the Provision for loan losses to net loan charge-offs (which
declined). Net Stable Funding Ratio is higher than 100% in all 3 years and increasing.
The second Basel III liquidity standard is the Net Stable Funding Ratio: a minimum
percentage of required stable funding that must be sourced from available stable
funding. As with the Liquidity Coverage Ratio, a ratio of 100% is the minimum
acceptable threshold.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following information about

Which of the following statements best describes the efficiency of money spent in
obtaining new premiums?

options.A: Insurer A is more efficient than Insurer B
options.B: Insurer B is more efficient than Insurer A
options.C: Both Insurer A and Insurer B are equally efficient

questions.explanation:
the underwriting expense ratios for Insurer A and Insurer B
are equal, at 40%. Therefore, they are equally efficient. Underwriting expense ratio
= Underwriting expense/Net premiums written. This ratio measures the efficiency
of money spent in obtaining new premiums. A lower ratio indicates higher success.
Since the ratios are equal, both Insurer A and Insurer B are equally efficient in terms
of money spent in obtaining new premiums.

questions.question:
An analyst gathers the following information (in $
millions) of an insurer and evaluates the overall efficiency of its operation:

The insurer's combined ratio is:
questions.explanation:
120% is the combined ratio of the insurer.
Combined ratio = Loss and loss adjustment expense ratio + Underwriting expense
ratio.
Loss and loss adjustment expense ratio = (Loss expense + Loss adjustment
expense)/Net premiums earned.
Underwriting expense ratio = Underwriting expense/Net premiums written.

Portfolio Management: Practice Pack

1. [Analysis of Active Portfolio Management] Which of the following statements
about the use of benchmarks in measuring the value added by active portfolio
management is most accurate?
A. Benchmark weights should be verifiable ex ante, and return data should be
timely ex post
B. Securities in the active portfolio should always be a subset of the securities
in the benchmark portfolio
C. Non-float-adjusted indexes are better than float-adjusted indexes for use
as benchmarks, as the total value of the securities is considered
2. [Analysis of Active Portfolio Management] An investor gathers the following
information about their portfolio:

The portfolio has generated positive alpha over the benchmark from:
A. asset allocation decisions only.
B. security selection decisions only.
C. both asset allocation decisions and security selection decisions.
3. [Analysis of Active Portfolio Management] The information ratio of a portfolio
of risky assets will remain unchanged when:
A. cash is added to the portfolio.
B. the use of leverage in the portfolio changes.
C. each active weight in the portfolio is multiplied by the same constant.
4. [Analysis of Active Portfolio Management] An actively managed fund has a
Sharpe ratio of 2.5. If the Sharpe ratio of the benchmark is 1.5, the fund's
information ratio is closest to:
A. 1.0.
B. 2.0.
C. 2.9.
5. [Analysis of Active Portfolio Management] According to the fundamental law of
active management, if breadth increased by a factor of four and active portfolio

risk decreased by a factor of four, all else being equal, the expected active
return would:
A. halve.
B. remain unchanged.
C. double.
6. [Analysis of Active Portfolio Management] In active portfolio management, the
transfer coefficient is the correlation between:
A. realized active returns and active weights.
B. forecasted active returns and active weights.
C. forecasted active returns and realized active returns.
7. [Analysis of Active Portfolio Management] An actively managed strategy uses
individual stock selection with a benchmark composed of 16 securities. The skill
of the investor is represented by an information coefficient of 0.12 and the
portfolio's active risk target is 5.0% per year. If the strategy's breadth is
equal to the number of benchmark securities and the investor's forecasts can
be implemented without constraints, the expected active return for this
strategy is closest to:
A. 2.4%.
B. 6.9%.
C. 9.6%.
8. [Analysis of Active Portfolio Management] If a portfolio has positive expected
active returns, the ex-ante information ratio will most likely increase with an
increase in the:
A. breadth of the portfolio.
B. active risk of the portfolio.
C. number of binding constraints on the portfolio.
9. [Analysis of Active Portfolio Management] An analyst gathers the following
information about two portfolios:

The expected active return of Portfolio 1 is:
A. less than the expected active return of Portfolio 2.
B. equal to the expected active return of Portfolio 2.
C. greater than the expected active return of Portfolio 2.


10. [Analysis of Active Portfolio Management] A fund's CIO makes the following
changes to the fund's investment strategy:

In aggregate, the changes will:
A. reduce the expected active return of the fund.
B. have no impact on the expected active return of the fund.
C. ncrease the expected active return of the fund.
11. [Analysis of Active Portfolio Management] Breadth can be greater than the
number of securities when:
A. derivatives are used for hedging in the portfolio.
B. return forecasts from period to period are correlated.
C. a decision is made to overweight all stocks in a given industry.
12. [Analysis of Active Portfolio Management] The breadth of an investment
strategy can be significantly higher than the number of individual assets for:
A. an arbitrage portfolio where derivatives are used.
B. a long-only stock portfolio with no systematic risk.
C. a long-only bond portfolio consisting of US Treasuries.
13. [Using Multifactor Models] With respect to the arbitrage pricing theory, an
arbitrage opportunity exists if a trader can earn a positive net profit with:
A. no risk and no net investment.
B. some risk and no net investment.
C. no risk and a positive net investment.
14. [Using Multifactor Models] The arbitrage pricing theory:
A. assumes that a single-factor model describes asset returns.
B. assumes that well-diversified portfolios provide arbitrage opportunities.
C. does not prescribe the identity of risk factors related to generating
returns.
15. [Using Multifactor Models] An analyst gathers the following information on the
expected return of three well-diversified portfolios and their sensitivity to a


Based on a one-factor arbitrage pricing theory model, an arbitrage opportunity
can be exploited by:
A. buying $400 of Portfolio X and $600 of Portfolio Y, and selling short $1,000
of Portfolio Z.
B. buying $500 of Portfolio X and $500 of Portfolio Y, and selling short $1,000
of Portfolio Z.
C. selling short $400 of Portfolio X and $600 of Portfolio Y, and buying $1,000
of Portfolio Z.
16. [Using Multifactor Models] An analyst gathers the following information about
three portfolios:

If Portfolio 1 and Portfolio 2 are priced correctly according to a one-factor
arbitrage pricing theory model, it can be concluded that Portfolio 3 is:
A. undervalued relative to its factor risk.
B. correctly valued relative to its factor risk.
C. overvalued relative to its factor risk.
17. [Using Multifactor Models] An analyst gathers the following information about
two well-diversified portfolios in an economy satisfying a one-factor arbitrage
pricing theory model:

The expected returns reflect a 1-year investment horizon and all investors
agree on the expected returns of these portfolios. The return on the risk-free
asset is closest to:
A. 3.0%.
B. 4.0%.
C. 5.0%.


18. [Using Multifactor Models] The returns of stocks X and Y are given by a
macroeconomic factor model and expressed as follows:
RX = 0.08 – 0.05F1 + 0.03F2 + εX
RY = 0.16 + 0.02F1 + 0.04F2 + εY,
where F1 and F2 reflect surprises in two macroeconomic factors. If a portfolio
has one quarter of its investment in Stock X and the rest in Stock Y, the
portfolio's expected return is closest to:
A. 12%.
B. 14%.
C. 18%.
19. [Using Multifactor Models] Which of the following statements about
fundamental factor models is most accurate?
A. The factors are stated as returns rather than return surprises in relation to
predicted values
B. The factor (surprise) series is developed first and then the factor
sensitivities are estimated using regressions
C. They can be easily applied to various asset classes, including fixed income, as
no asset-class-specific tuning is required
20. [Using Multifactor Models] Which of the following statements about factor
models is most accurate?
A. In a statistical factor model, the factors are defined by portfolio weights
B. In a macroeconomic factor model, the factors are consensus forecasts of
macroeconomic variables
C. In a fundamental factor model, the factors are company-specific and do not
include macroeconomic factors
21. [Using Multifactor Models] An analyst gathers the following information on
three well-diversified funds, where the returns and tracking errors are based
on a five-factor macroeconomic model:

The fund with the most favorable information ratio is:
A. Fund X.
B. Fund Y.
C. Fund Z.

22. [Using Multifactor Models] With respect to risk attribution in active portfolio
management, tracking error is defined as the:
A. standard deviation of the differences between a portfolio's return and its
benchmark return.
B. quare of the standard deviation of the differences between a portfolio's
return and its benchmark return.
C. difference between the standard deviation of a portfolio's return and the
standard deviation of its benchmark return.
23. [Using Multifactor Models] A portfolio manager gathers the following
information about three well-diversified portfolios and their sensitivities to
four factors using a macroeconomic factor model:

The portfolio best suited to hedge an existing short exposure to Factor 3 is:
A. Portfolio X.
B. Portfolio Y.
C. Portfolio Z.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements
about the use of benchmarks in measuring the value added by active portfolio
management is most accurate?

options.A: Benchmark weights should be verifiable ex ante, and return data should be timely ex post
options.B: Securities in the active portfolio should always be a subset of the securities in the benchmark portfolio
options.C: Non-float-adjusted indexes are better than float-adjusted indexes for use as benchmarks, as the total value of the securities is considered

questions.explanation:
a benchmark or passive portfolio should have a number of
qualities to serve as a relevant comparison for active management, one of which
is that benchmark weights are verifiable ex ante, and return data are timely ex
post.

2. A is Correct because, using a performance attribution system for just two asset
classes, stocks and bonds, gives
RA = (ΔwstocksRB,stocks + ΔwbondsRB,bonds) +
(wP,stocksRA,stocks + wP,bondsRA,bonds),
where R = return, w = weight, P = portfolio, B = benchmark, hence RA = RP – RB =
alpha.
The first (parenthetical) term is the value added from the asset allocation
decision. The second term is the value added from security selection within the
stock and bond portfolios.
Hence, asset allocation alpha = [(80% – 60%) × 13.0%] + [(20% – 40%) × 4.0%] =
1.8% and security selection alpha = [80% × (11.0% – 13.0%)] + [20% × (5.0% –
4.0%)] = –1.4%.
Therefore, positive alpha has only been generated from asset allocation
decisions.
3. C is Correct because, if each active weight is multiplied by some constant, c,
then the active return on the altered portfolio, RC, is cRA.
Similarly, the active risk of the altered portfolio is cσA, so the information ratio
of the altered portfolio is
IRC = cRA/cσA = RA/σA = IR,
the same as that of the actively managed portfolio with no proportional increase
in the active weights.

questions.question:
An actively managed fund has a
Sharpe ratio of 2.5. If the Sharpe ratio of the benchmark is 1.5, the fund's
information ratio is closest to:
questions.explanation:
the squared Sharpe ratio of an actively managed portfolio
(P) is equal to the squared Sharpe ratio of the benchmark (B) plus the
information ratio squared:
SRP2 = SRB2 + IR2.
Substituting,
(2.5)2 = (1.5)2 + IR2
IR = [(2.5)2 – (1.5)2]1/2 = 2.0.

questions.question:
According to the fundamental law of
active management, if breadth increased by a factor of four and active portfolio


risk decreased by a factor of four, all else being equal, the expected active
return would:
questions.explanation:
the expected active return E(RA) = (TC)(IC)(√BR)σA,
where TC = transfer coefficient, IC = information coefficient, BR = breadth, and
σA = active portfolio risk.
Since E(RA) is proportional to √BR, if BR increases by a factor of 4, then E(RA)
increases by a factor of 2. Also, E(RA) is proportional to σA, so if σAdecreases
by a factor of 4, then E(RA) decreases by a factor of 4 as well.
Combining both effects, if BR increases by a factor of 4 and σA decreases by a
factor of 4, then E(RA) increases by a factor of 2/4 = 1/2, i.e. E(RA) halves.

EdZeb Institute

questions.question:
In active portfolio management, the
transfer coefficient is the correlation between:

options.A: realized active returns and active weights
options.B: forecasted active returns and active weights
options.C: forecasted active returns and realized active returns

questions.explanation:
the correlation between any set of active weights, Δwi, and
forecasted active returns, µi, measures the degree to which the investor’s
forecasts are translated into active weights, called the transfer coefficient
(TC).

questions.question:
An actively managed strategy uses
individual stock selection with a benchmark composed of 16 securities. The skill
of the investor is represented by an information coefficient of 0.12 and the
portfolio's active risk target is 5.0% per year. If the strategy's breadth is
equal to the number of benchmark securities and the investor's forecasts can
be implemented without constraints, the expected active return for this
strategy is closest to:
questions.explanation:
the information ratio of the unconstrained strategy is
given by IR = (IC)(√BR) and expected active return is calculated as
E(RA) = (IR)σA = (IC)(√BR)σA = (0.12)(√16)(5.0%) = 2.4%.

questions.question:
If a portfolio has positive expected
active returns, the ex-ante information ratio will most likely increase with an
increase in the:

options.C: number of binding constraints on the portfolio

questions.explanation:
a portfolio's information ratio, E(RA)/σA, can be expressed
as the product of just three terms: IR = (TC)(IC)√BR, where IR = information
ratio, TC = transfer coefficient, IC = information coefficient, and BR = breadth
of the portfolio. Hence, if the breadth of the portfolio increases, the
information ratio will increase.
